ml-engineer.title: Python & Math Foundations
ml-engineer.description: ML is applied math in code. You need fluent Python and enough linear algebra, calculus, and statistics to understand why algorithms work — not just how to call them.
topics: Python — functions, classes, list comprehensions, generators
Linear algebra — vectors, matrices, dot product, eigenvalues
Statistics — mean, variance, normal distribution, Bayes' theorem
ml-engineer.challenge: Implement gradient descent from scratch in NumPy — fit a linear regression model to a synthetic dataset without using any ML library.

children.title: Python Data Structures & OOP
children.description: ML pipelines rely on clean, reusable Python code. Without a solid grasp of classes, generators, and comprehensions your code becomes brittle and hard to debug at scale. Understanding memory layout helps you avoid costly copies when feeding data to models.

children.title: NumPy Vectorized Operations
children.description: NumPy's broadcasting rules let you write loop-free math that runs at C speed. Misunderstanding axis semantics or shape alignment causes silent bugs that produce wrong gradients without raising an error. Mastering it is a prerequisite for every numerical library in the ecosystem.

children.title: Linear Algebra for ML
children.description: Every weight matrix, attention head, and PCA transformation is linear algebra. If you cannot reason about matrix shapes and eigenvalues you will misread model architectures and misinterpret dimensionality reduction results. This is the single most leveraged math topic in ML.

children.title: Probability & Statistics
children.description: Loss functions, confidence intervals, hypothesis tests, and Bayesian priors are all statistics. Without this foundation you cannot tell whether a model improvement is real or noise, and you will misuse metrics like accuracy on imbalanced datasets. Statistics is how ML connects to the real world.

children.title: Gradients & the Chain Rule
children.description: Backpropagation is nothing but the chain rule applied repeatedly through a computation graph. If you do not understand how gradients flow through composed functions you cannot debug vanishing gradients, exploding updates, or incorrect custom loss implementations. This is the engine behind every neural network.

children.title: Optimization Intuition
children.description: Gradient descent, momentum, and adaptive learning rates all come from optimization theory. Not understanding the loss landscape — saddle points, local minima, learning rate schedules — leads to models that fail to converge or overfit aggressively. Intuition here separates practitioners from button-pushers.

children.title: Jupyter Notebook Best Practices
children.description: Notebooks are the default ML workspace but hidden state and non-linear execution cause reproducibility failures that are hard to track down. Learning cell ordering discipline and when to graduate code to modules prevents hours of debugging. Good notebook hygiene is what separates experiments you can share from ones you cannot reproduce yourself.

resources.label: Ten Simple Rules for Reproducible Research in Jupyter Notebooks

children.title: Virtual Environments & Dependency Management
children.description: ML projects accumulate conflicting CUDA, Python, and library versions that silently produce wrong results. Without isolated environments a single pip install can break every project on the machine. Proper dependency pinning is also the first step toward reproducible, deployable models.

ml-engineer.title: Data Manipulation & EDA
ml-engineer.description: Real-world data is messy. Learn to load, clean, transform, and visualize data with Pandas and Matplotlib — skills you'll use in every single ML project.
topics: Pandas — DataFrames, Series, indexing, groupby, merge
Data cleaning — missing values, duplicates, outliers
EDA — correlation matrices, distributions, pair plots
ml-engineer.challenge: Pick a public Kaggle dataset, perform a full EDA — handle missing values, engineer 2+ new features, and produce 5 informative visualizations that reveal insights.

children.title: Pandas for Tabular Data
children.description: Real-world datasets are messy, and Pandas is the primary tool for cleaning, reshaping, and joining them before any model sees the data. Mistakes in groupby, merge semantics, or handling NaN values propagate silently through pipelines and degrade model quality. Speed with Pandas directly determines how fast you can iterate on feature engineering.

children.title: Missing Data & Feature Engineering
children.description: How you handle missing values and engineer features is often the biggest lever on model accuracy — more than algorithm choice. Imputation strategies must match the missingness mechanism (MCAR vs MAR vs MNAR) or they introduce bias. Thoughtful feature engineering encodes domain knowledge the model cannot learn on its own.

resources.label: Feature Engineering for Machine Learning (O'Reilly excerpt)

children.title: Descriptive Statistics & Distribution Analysis
children.description: Before training any model you need to understand the data's distribution, skewness, outliers, and class balance. Ignoring these leads to models that overfit to artifacts, predict the wrong class most often, or collapse to the mean. EDA is how you generate hypotheses that guide feature work and model selection.

children.title: Correlation & Multivariate Analysis
children.description: Correlation matrices, pair plots, and mutual information reveal relationships between features that inform dimensionality reduction and model selection. Ignoring multicollinearity can destabilize linear models and make feature importance scores misleading. Multivariate analysis is where you find the signal before the model even trains.

children.title: Matplotlib Fundamentals
children.description: Matplotlib is the foundation every other Python visualization library builds on, and understanding its figure/axes object model lets you customize plots that higher-level APIs cannot produce. Misconfigured axes scales or color maps can make a model's performance look better or worse than it really is. Visualization is not cosmetic — it is how you find bugs in data and models.

children.title: Seaborn for Statistical Plots
children.description: Seaborn wraps Matplotlib with statistical visualization primitives like violin plots, heatmaps, and regression lines that communicate distributional information naturally. Using bar charts where violin plots belong hides variance and leads to wrong conclusions about model behavior. The right chart type is part of a rigorous ML workflow.

children.title: Reproducible EDA Pipelines
children.description: An EDA that cannot be rerun produces insights that cannot be verified and transformations that cannot be reapplied to new data. Parameterizing notebooks with tools like Papermill lets you run the same analysis on multiple datasets automatically. Reproducibility at the EDA stage prevents data leakage bugs from sneaking into production.

children.title: Profiling Large DataFrames
children.description: When datasets have hundreds of columns and millions of rows manual EDA is infeasible. Automated profiling tools generate distribution plots, correlation heatmaps, and missing-value reports in seconds. Skipping profiling on large datasets means you start modeling with unknown data quality issues that surface only after expensive training runs.

ml-engineer.title: Classical Machine Learning
ml-engineer.description: Before deep learning, master the classical algorithms. Linear regression, decision trees, ensembles — these still power most production ML systems and are faster to train and explain.
topics: Supervised learning — linear/logistic regression, decision trees, SVMs
Ensemble methods — Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost
Model evaluation — accuracy, precision, recall, F1, ROC-AUC
ml-engineer.challenge: Enter a Kaggle tabular competition (e.g. Titanic or House Prices) — train at least 3 models, tune hyperparameters, and submit a solution that beats the baseline.

children.title: Estimator API & Pipelines
children.description: Scikit-learn's fit/transform/predict contract is the design pattern the entire classical ML ecosystem follows. Building pipelines that chain preprocessing and modeling prevents data leakage from validation sets — the single most common source of inflated offline metrics. Understanding the API deeply also makes switching estimators trivial.

children.title: Model Selection & Hyperparameter Tuning
children.description: Choosing a model without cross-validation is guessing, and tuning hyperparameters on the test set invalidates your evaluation. Grid search and randomized search systematically explore hyperparameter space while keeping held-out data truly held out. Poor model selection is why models that look good offline perform badly in production.

children.title: Random Forests & Gradient Boosting
children.description: Ensemble methods are among the highest-performing algorithms on tabular data and win most structured-data Kaggle competitions. Understanding how bagging reduces variance and boosting reduces bias helps you diagnose whether a model is underfitting or overfitting. Without this intuition you tune parameters blindly.

children.title: XGBoost & LightGBM
children.description: XGBoost and LightGBM are the go-to algorithms for production tabular ML due to their speed, regularization built-ins, and native handling of missing values. Misunderstanding their tree construction strategies leads to suboptimal max_depth and learning_rate choices that leave significant performance on the table. They also power most production recommendation and fraud-detection systems.

children.title: Metrics Beyond Accuracy
children.description: Accuracy is misleading on imbalanced datasets, and choosing the wrong metric means optimizing for the wrong thing in production. Precision-recall tradeoffs, AUC-ROC, and F1 are tied to real business costs like false negatives in fraud detection. Understanding which metric matches your problem is a prerequisite for making any model improvement claim.

children.title: Cross-Validation Strategies
children.description: Standard k-fold fails for time-series data because future data leaks into training folds, and stratified folds are required for class-imbalanced problems. Using the wrong cross-validation strategy produces an optimistic offline evaluation that does not transfer to production. Understanding fold strategies is how you build trust in your offline metrics.

children.title: Feature Importance & SHAP Values
children.description: Tree-based feature importance is biased toward high-cardinality features; SHAP values give a theoretically grounded explanation of each prediction. Without interpretability you cannot validate that the model is learning real signal rather than spurious correlations in the training data. Explainability is also a regulatory requirement in finance, healthcare, and hiring.

children.title: Calibration & Confidence
children.description: A model that outputs 0.9 probability should be right 90% of the time — but most classifiers are miscalibrated by default. Uncalibrated probabilities break downstream decision thresholds and risk calculations in production. Platt scaling and isotonic regression are standard fixes that every practitioner should know.

ml-engineer.title: Deep Learning with PyTorch
ml-engineer.description: PyTorch is the leading deep learning framework. Understand how neural networks learn, build architectures from scratch, and train models on GPU.
topics: Neural network anatomy — layers, activations, loss functions
Training loop — forward pass, loss, backward pass, optimizer step
CNNs — conv layers, pooling, feature maps
Transfer learning — torchvision pretrained models, fine-tuning

resources.label: fast.ai — Practical Deep Learning for Coders
Andrej Karpathy — Neural Networks: Zero to Hero

ml-engineer.challenge: Train a CNN image classifier on CIFAR-10 with PyTorch — implement the full training loop from scratch, achieve >85% accuracy, and visualize misclassified examples.

children.title: Tensors & Autograd
children.description: PyTorch tensors are the primitive that everything else is built on, and autograd is the automatic differentiation engine that computes gradients for you. Misunderstanding in-place operations, detach, or gradient accumulation causes wrong gradients that silently corrupt training. Fluency here is required before you can write or debug any custom model.

children.title: nn.Module & Training Loop
children.description: The nn.Module abstraction is how PyTorch models are composed from layers, and the training loop is where forward pass, loss computation, backward pass, and optimizer step all connect. Getting any step out of order — like calling optimizer.step() before loss.backward() — produces no error but no learning either. Mastering this loop gives you full control over every aspect of training.

resources.label: PyTorch Build the Neural Network Tutorial

children.title: CNN Architecture Fundamentals
children.description: Convolutional layers exploit spatial locality and weight sharing to learn visual features with far fewer parameters than fully connected layers. Without understanding receptive fields, stride, and padding you cannot design architectures that see the right context or debug shape mismatches between layers. CNNs remain the backbone of most vision production systems.

children.title: Transfer Learning with Pretrained Models
children.description: Training CNNs from scratch requires millions of labeled images and weeks of GPU time; transfer learning from ImageNet-pretrained models achieves strong results on small datasets in hours. Misunderstanding which layers to freeze versus fine-tune leads to either catastrophic forgetting or failure to adapt to the new domain. Transfer learning is the default approach in production vision tasks.

children.title: CUDA & Device Management
children.description: Moving tensors between CPU and GPU incorrectly is the most common source of runtime errors in PyTorch, and mismatched devices cause cryptic errors that beginners spend hours debugging. Understanding GPU memory limits helps you choose batch sizes that avoid OOM crashes. Efficient GPU utilization is the difference between a 1-hour and a 10-hour training run.

children.title: Mixed Precision Training
children.description: FP16 mixed precision training halves GPU memory use and doubles throughput on modern hardware with negligible accuracy loss. Without it you are leaving the most impactful free optimization on the table. PyTorch's torch.cuda.amp makes this a handful of lines of code once you understand why loss scaling is necessary.

children.title: Dropout, BatchNorm & Weight Decay
children.description: Dropout and batch normalization are the two most universally applied regularization techniques in deep learning, yet their interaction — especially at inference time — is a frequent source of bugs. Forgetting model.eval() disables these correctly and causes different behavior between training and serving. Understanding them properly means fewer mysterious accuracy gaps between dev and production.

children.title: Gradient Flow & Loss Debugging
children.description: Exploding or vanishing gradients kill training silently — the loss plateaus or becomes NaN and it is not obvious why. Monitoring gradient norms, using gradient clipping, and checking for dead ReLUs are essential debugging skills. TensorBoard and PyTorch hooks make gradient inspection straightforward once you know what to look for.

ml-engineer.title: NLP & Transformers
ml-engineer.description: NLP is one of the hottest areas in ML. Learn to work with text data, understand the Transformer architecture, and fine-tune pre-trained models with HuggingFace.
topics: Text preprocessing — tokenization, padding, special tokens
Word embeddings — Word2Vec, GloVe, contextual embeddings
ml-engineer.challenge: Fine-tune DistilBERT on a sentiment analysis dataset (SST-2 or your own), evaluate with F1, and expose it as a FastAPI endpoint.

children.title: Attention Mechanism & Self-Attention
children.description: Self-attention is the core operation that lets transformers model long-range dependencies without the sequential bottleneck of RNNs. Without understanding queries, keys, and values you cannot read architecture papers, debug attention scores, or reason about what a model attends to. Attention is the foundation of every modern NLP and multimodal system.

resources.label: Attention Is All You Need (original paper)

children.title: Tokenization & Embeddings
children.description: How text is tokenized determines model vocabulary, out-of-vocabulary handling, and multilingual capability. Using the wrong tokenizer for a pretrained model produces garbage token IDs that cause immediate degradation. Understanding subword algorithms like BPE and WordPiece is required for any serious NLP work.

children.title: BERT Pretraining & Architecture
children.description: BERT's bidirectional masked language modeling pretraining gives it context from both directions, which is why it outperforms unidirectional models on understanding tasks. Knowing the difference between encoder-only, decoder-only, and encoder-decoder architectures tells you which model family fits your task without trial and error. This shapes every architecture decision in NLP.

children.title: HuggingFace Transformers Library
children.description: The Transformers library provides a unified API to load, fine-tune, and serve hundreds of pretrained models across NLP, vision, and audio. Without knowing its pipeline, AutoModel, and Trainer abstractions you reinvent the wheel for every task. It is the standard interface between research models and production NLP engineering.

children.title: Full Fine-tuning vs Parameter-Efficient Methods
children.description: Full fine-tuning updates all weights and requires the same GPU memory as pretraining, which is often infeasible. LoRA and prefix tuning achieve near-equivalent performance by updating only a tiny fraction of parameters, making fine-tuning accessible on a single GPU. Choosing the wrong method wastes compute budget or produces a model too large to serve.

children.title: Dataset Preparation for NLP Fine-tuning
children.description: NLP datasets require careful preprocessing — padding, truncation, attention mask alignment, and label encoding — before they can be fed to a Trainer. Getting any of these wrong produces training that appears to work but produces nonsensical outputs or poor generalization. The HuggingFace Datasets library standardizes this while handling memory mapping for large corpora.

children.title: NLP Metrics: BLEU, ROUGE, BERTScore
children.description: BLEU and ROUGE measure n-gram overlap which correlates poorly with semantic quality for open-ended generation tasks; BERTScore uses contextual embeddings to capture meaning more accurately. Choosing the wrong metric leads you to optimize for surface-level text patterns rather than actual quality. Metric selection is how you define 'better' in NLP, and it must match the downstream use case.

children.title: Benchmark Datasets & GLUE
children.description: GLUE and SuperGLUE standardize NLP evaluation across tasks so you can compare models fairly and track progress against the field. Without running standard benchmarks you do not know if your fine-tuned model is competitive or whether improvements on your small dataset are generalizable. Benchmarks also reveal task-specific weaknesses invisible on custom evaluation sets.

ml-engineer.title: MLOps & Experiment Tracking
ml-engineer.description: Production ML is 90% engineering. Track experiments, version your data and models, and build reproducible pipelines so your team can collaborate on ML projects.
topics: MLflow — tracking, model registry, projects, artifacts
Weights & Biases — experiment tracking, hyperparameter sweeps
DVC — data versioning, pipelines, remote storage
ML pipelines with Prefect or Airflow
ml-engineer.challenge: Set up MLflow for your sentiment model — log all hyperparameters, metrics, and model artifacts. Use DVC to version the dataset, and run a W&B sweep to find the best learning rate.

children.title: MLflow Tracking & Model Registry
children.description: Without experiment tracking you cannot reproduce a result that happened two weeks ago or compare 50 hyperparameter runs systematically. MLflow logs parameters, metrics, artifacts, and model versions in a queryable store that turns ad-hoc experiments into a searchable history. The Model Registry adds approval workflows and stage transitions that production deployment processes require.

children.title: Weights & Biases (W&B)
children.description: W&B provides richer real-time visualization of training runs, system metrics, and gradient histograms than local TensorBoard and syncs across team members automatically. Missing a training instability because you were not monitoring GPU memory or gradient norms in real time costs hours of wasted compute. W&B sweeps also automate hyperparameter search across distributed agents.

children.title: DVC — Data Version Control
children.description: Git tracks code but not large datasets and model files; DVC extends Git to version these artifacts stored in remote storage like S3 or GCS. Without data versioning you cannot reproduce past experiments because you do not know exactly which dataset snapshot trained a given model. DVC pipelines also cache intermediate stages so reruns skip already-computed steps.

children.title: Dataset Lineage & Provenance
children.description: Knowing exactly which raw data, transformations, and splits produced a given training set is required to audit models for bias, debug performance regressions, and comply with data governance regulations. Without lineage tracking a change in upstream data silently corrupts future models. Dataset cards and versioned feature stores are how mature ML teams manage this.

children.title: Optuna — Bayesian Optimization
children.description: Random search wastes trials on poor hyperparameter regions; Optuna's TPE sampler uses past trial results to propose configurations more likely to improve the objective. Pruning unpromising trials early with Optuna's integration hooks can reduce total search time by 10x. Systematic HPO is what separates a tuned model from a model that happens to work.

children.title: Early Stopping & Callbacks
children.description: Training without early stopping wastes GPU time on epochs that only increase overfitting, and missing a learning rate plateau can cause a model to stall for dozens of unnecessary epochs. Callbacks let you inject custom logic — checkpointing, metric logging, dynamic LR scheduling — at every stage of training without rewriting the training loop. They are the hook system that makes training loops composable.

children.title: Seeding & Deterministic Training
children.description: Without fixed random seeds the same code produces different results on every run, making it impossible to attribute an improvement to a code change versus random chance. PyTorch's non-deterministic CUDA operations require explicit configuration to become reproducible and that configuration has a performance cost you must consciously accept. Reproducibility is a prerequisite for scientific rigor in ML.

children.title: Environment Reproducibility with Conda & Docker
children.description: A model that trains on your laptop but fails on the cluster almost always has an environment mismatch — different CUDA version, library build flags, or Python minor version. Docker images freeze the entire software stack so that any machine produces identical behavior. This is the foundation of reproducible MLOps pipelines.

ml-engineer.title: Model Deployment
ml-engineer.description: A model that isn't deployed doesn't deliver value. Package your model as a production REST API, optimize for inference speed, and deploy to the cloud.
topics: Model serving with FastAPI — async inference endpoint
Model optimization — ONNX export, quantization, pruning
A/B testing and shadow mode deployment

resources.label: Chip Huyen — Designing Machine Learning Systems (book)

ml-engineer.challenge: Deploy your fine-tuned sentiment model as a Dockerized FastAPI service — optimize with ONNX for 2x inference speedup, add a /health endpoint, and deploy to Fly.io.

children.title: FastAPI for ML Endpoints
children.description: FastAPI's async request handling, automatic OpenAPI schema generation, and Pydantic validation make it the standard framework for wrapping ML models in production REST APIs. Serving a model without input validation lets malformed requests reach the model and produce confusing errors or silent wrong predictions. FastAPI also makes it easy to add auth, rate limiting, and monitoring middleware.

children.title: TorchServe for PyTorch Models
children.description: TorchServe is PyTorch's native model server with built-in batching, multi-model management, versioning, and metrics — features that are tedious to build on top of a raw FastAPI server. Without batching, a model server processes one request at a time and leaves GPU utilization below 10%. Understanding TorchServe's handler API lets you customize preprocessing and postprocessing inside the serving runtime.

children.title: Docker for ML Deployment
children.description: Docker packages your model, dependencies, and serving code into an image that runs identically from a laptop to a Kubernetes pod in production. Without containerization environment drift between training and serving is the leading cause of serving-side bugs that do not appear in local testing. Multi-stage builds keep images small and reproducible.

children.title: Container Registries & CI/CD
children.description: Storing model-serving images in a registry like Docker Hub or AWS ECR with tagged versions enables rollback when a new model causes a regression in production. Without automated build and push pipelines, deployments are manual, error-prone, and undocumented. CI/CD for ML images is how teams ship model updates with the same rigor as software releases.

children.title: ONNX Export & Cross-Platform Inference
children.description: ONNX is the interchange format that lets you train in PyTorch and deploy to TensorRT, ONNX Runtime, or mobile runtimes without rewriting the model. Without it, switching inference backends requires porting model code and revalidating outputs. ONNX Runtime's graph optimizations typically provide 2-4x latency improvement over native PyTorch inference for free.

children.title: Quantization & Model Compression
children.description: Post-training quantization reduces model size by 4x and inference latency by 2-3x with minimal accuracy loss, enabling deployment to edge devices or reducing cloud inference costs. Skipping quantization means paying full GPU cost for serving when a quantized model on CPU would suffice. Knowing when accuracy loss is acceptable requires understanding where the model's precision actually matters.

children.title: Model Performance Monitoring
children.description: Models degrade silently in production as data distributions shift, and without monitoring you find out only when a business metric collapses. Tracking prediction distributions, latency percentiles, and error rates over time lets you catch drift before it causes customer-facing problems. Alerting on statistical drift is the difference between proactive and reactive ML operations.

children.title: Logging & Tracing in Serving
children.description: Structured logs with request IDs, model version, and prediction values are what you need when debugging a production incident or auditing model decisions for compliance. Without tracing you cannot correlate a user complaint to a specific model version and input. Integrating OpenTelemetry into your serving stack makes end-to-end tracing possible without heavy instrumentation.

ml-engineer.title: LLMs & Generative AI
ml-engineer.description: LLMs are reshaping what's possible in software. Learn to build AI-powered applications with LangChain, implement RAG for grounded generation, and work with vector databases.
topics: LLM APIs — OpenAI, Anthropic, Groq, HuggingFace Inference
Prompt engineering — system prompts, few-shot, chain-of-thought
RAG — embeddings, vector databases (Chroma, Qdrant, Pinecone)
ml-engineer.challenge: Build a RAG-powered Q&A system over a set of PDF documents — chunk and embed the docs, store in Chroma, and build a FastAPI endpoint that answers questions with citations.

children.title: OpenAI API & Prompt Engineering
children.description: The OpenAI API is the fastest path to building LLM-powered features, and prompt engineering determines whether the model follows instructions reliably or produces hallucinations. A poorly structured prompt wastes tokens, increases costs, and produces inconsistent outputs that break downstream parsing. Understanding system prompts, few-shot examples, and output formatting is the foundation of practical LLM application development.

children.title: LangChain for LLM Orchestration
children.description: LangChain provides chains, agents, and memory abstractions that connect LLMs to tools, data sources, and multi-step reasoning pipelines without rebuilding plumbing for every project. Without an orchestration framework, multi-step LLM workflows become tangled spaghetti that is hard to debug and impossible to swap models in. LangChain's ecosystem of integrations covers most production LLM patterns.

children.title: RAG Architecture & Chunking Strategies
children.description: RAG grounds LLM responses in retrieved documents, dramatically reducing hallucination on knowledge-intensive tasks. Chunk size and overlap are the most impactful RAG hyperparameters — chunks too small lose context, too large dilute relevance signals. Understanding the retrieve-then-generate pipeline is required before any production knowledge assistant can be built.

children.title: Vector Databases
children.description: Vector databases store and index high-dimensional embeddings for approximate nearest neighbor search at millisecond latency, which is what makes semantic retrieval in RAG systems possible at scale. Using a flat NumPy search works in prototypes but collapses at millions of documents. Understanding HNSW indexing and similarity metrics helps you tune retrieval precision vs recall for your use case.

children.title: Text Embeddings & Similarity
children.description: Embedding models map text into dense vectors where semantic similarity corresponds to geometric proximity, enabling search, clustering, and classification without labeled data. Using bag-of-words TF-IDF where sentence embeddings are appropriate misses paraphrases and synonyms that users actually write. The quality of embeddings is the ceiling of RAG retrieval quality.

children.title: FAISS for Efficient Similarity Search
children.description: FAISS implements GPU-accelerated exact and approximate nearest neighbor search that scales to billions of vectors where databases like Chroma would time out. Understanding when to use IVF vs HNSW indexes helps you balance memory, build time, and query speed. FAISS is the indexing backbone of many production RAG and recommendation systems.

children.title: Hallucination Detection & Faithfulness
children.description: LLMs confidently generate false information, and without automated faithfulness checks your RAG system will serve wrong answers at scale. Metrics like RAGAS measure answer relevance, context precision, and faithfulness against retrieved documents. Catching hallucinations before deployment requires a structured evaluation harness, not spot-checking.

children.title: LLM Cost & Latency Optimization
children.description: LLM API calls are expensive and slow at scale — a naive implementation that calls GPT-4 for every user message will blow the budget. Caching semantically equivalent queries, routing simple requests to cheaper models, and streaming responses are standard techniques. Without cost awareness an LLM feature that works in demos becomes uneconomical in production.

ml-engineer.title: Large-Scale Distributed Training
ml-engineer.description: Train large models efficiently across multiple GPUs and nodes using data parallelism, model parallelism, and ZeRO optimizer stages. Master mixed-precision training and gradient checkpointing to push throughput while staying within memory budgets.
topics: Data parallelism with DistributedDataParallel and FSDP
Model parallelism and pipeline parallelism strategies
Mixed-precision training with bfloat16 and fp16
Multi-node training with NCCL and RDMA networking
Profiling GPU utilization with PyTorch Profiler and Nsight

resources.label: Efficient Large-Scale Training - Hugging Face Course
Distributed Machine Learning Patterns by Yuan Tang

ml-engineer.challenge: Fine-tune a 7B parameter transformer on a multi-label classification task using DeepSpeed ZeRO-3 across two GPUs with bf16 mixed precision, log throughput (tokens per second) and peak memory usage per stage.

children.title: Data Parallelism & DDP
children.description: PyTorch DistributedDataParallel replicates the model on every GPU and synchronizes gradients via all-reduce, providing near-linear scaling with the number of GPUs for batch-bound training. Using DataParallel instead of DDP is a common beginner mistake that underutilizes multi-GPU setups due to GIL bottlenecks. Understanding gradient synchronization is prerequisite to debugging slow distributed runs.

children.title: HuggingFace Accelerate
children.description: Accelerate abstracts away the boilerplate of DDP, FSDP, and mixed precision so the same training script runs on a single GPU, multi-GPU, or multi-node cluster with one config change. Without it, porting a training script from one hardware setup to another requires significant rewriting. It is the standard abstraction for scalable training in the HuggingFace ecosystem.

children.title: DeepSpeed & ZeRO Optimization
children.description: ZeRO partitions optimizer states, gradients, and parameters across GPUs, allowing models with billions of parameters to train on hardware that would otherwise run out of memory. Without ZeRO, training a 7B parameter model requires tensor parallelism across dozens of GPUs; with ZeRO Stage 3 it fits on a fraction of that. Understanding which ZeRO stage to use requires knowing where your memory bottleneck actually is.

children.title: Model Parallelism & Pipeline Parallelism
children.description: When a model is too large to fit on a single GPU even with ZeRO, model parallelism splits layers across GPUs and pipeline parallelism staggers micro-batches to keep all stages busy. Naive model parallelism leaves upstream GPUs idle while downstream ones compute, wasting half the hardware. Understanding bubble overhead and micro-batch scheduling is required to achieve good GPU utilization in pipeline setups.

children.title: CUDA Programming Concepts
children.description: Understanding CUDA kernels, warps, and memory hierarchy helps you read profiler output and reason about why a custom operation is slow or why a library call has poor GPU utilization. Without this, PyTorch Profiler output is uninterpretable noise. Even ML engineers who never write CUDA kernels need this knowledge to choose the right data layout and batch size for peak throughput.

children.title: PyTorch Profiler & NVIDIA Nsight
children.description: Profiling reveals whether training is compute-bound, memory-bound, or communication-bound — three problems that require completely different fixes. Guessing which optimization to apply wastes days on changes that have no effect on the actual bottleneck. PyTorch Profiler and Nsight Systems give you the evidence to make targeted, high-impact optimization decisions.

children.title: NCCL & Collective Operations
children.description: NCCL implements all-reduce, broadcast, and scatter-gather over NVLink and InfiniBand — the communication backbone of every multi-GPU training framework. Misconfigured NCCL timeouts or wrong network interface selection causes training hangs that appear as dead processes with no error message. Understanding ring all-reduce complexity helps you predict how communication overhead scales with GPU count.

children.title: Gradient Checkpointing
children.description: Gradient checkpointing trades compute for memory by discarding intermediate activations during the forward pass and recomputing them during backward, enabling training of models 4-8x larger than VRAM would otherwise allow. Not knowing this technique means hitting OOM errors and concluding a model is untrainable when it actually is. It is a standard technique for training transformers beyond a few hundred million parameters.

ml-engineer.title: Production ML Systems and Responsible AI
ml-engineer.description: Build robust production ML pipelines that include feature stores, automated retraining triggers, model governance workflows, and bias monitoring. Apply responsible AI practices including fairness auditing, explainability, and compliance documentation.
topics: Feature store design and online/offline serving with Feast
Model governance and approval workflows in MLflow
Automated retraining pipelines triggered by data drift
Data quality checks with Great Expectations in pipelines
Fairness metrics and bias auditing techniques
Model explainability with SHAP and LIME
ML compliance documentation and model cards

resources.label: Designing Machine Learning Systems by Chip Huyen

ml-engineer.challenge: Build a retraining pipeline that ingests features from Feast, detects distribution drift with Evidently, triggers a new MLflow experiment when drift exceeds a threshold, and generates a model card before promotion to production.

children.title: Feature Stores with Feast
children.description: Feature stores decouple feature computation from model training and serving, ensuring that the features seen at inference time are identical to those used during training. Feature-training-serving skew is one of the most common and hardest-to-detect bugs in production ML systems. Feast provides a point-in-time correct retrieval API that prevents future data from leaking into historical training windows.

children.title: Data Quality with Great Expectations
children.description: Great Expectations lets you define expectations about data schema, value ranges, and distributions that run automatically in pipelines and alert when upstream data changes silently break assumptions. Without data validation, a change in an upstream table format silently trains the next model on garbage and the failure only surfaces after deployment. Data contracts are how mature ML teams prevent data-induced model failures.

children.title: Bias Detection & Fairness Metrics
children.description: Models trained on historical data inherit and often amplify societal biases, and deploying them without fairness auditing causes discriminatory outcomes in hiring, lending, and healthcare. Metrics like demographic parity, equalized odds, and individual fairness measure different notions of fairness that can conflict with each other. Choosing the right fairness criterion requires understanding the social context of the deployment.

children.title: Model Cards & Documentation
children.description: Model cards standardize what information accompanies a deployed model — intended use, performance across demographic groups, limitations, and potential misuse cases. Without documentation, downstream users make uninformed deployment decisions and regulators have no audit trail. Model cards are increasingly required by law in high-stakes domains and by major ML platforms for public models.

children.title: Orchestration with Airflow & Prefect
children.description: ML pipelines that run as cron scripts fail silently, have no retry logic, and cannot be monitored or rerun from a specific step. Workflow orchestrators like Airflow and Prefect provide DAG-based scheduling, dependency management, observability, and alerting that turn fragile scripts into reliable data products. Pipeline failures that go undetected for days are a direct consequence of skipping orchestration.

children.title: Kubernetes for ML Workloads
children.description: Kubernetes autoscales serving replicas under load, isolates model versions in separate pods, and integrates with GPU node pools for efficient resource sharing across teams. Without container orchestration, scaling a model from 10 to 10,000 requests per second requires manual intervention and causes downtime. Kubeflow and Seldon extend Kubernetes specifically for ML deployment patterns.

children.title: Differential Privacy & Data Anonymization
children.description: Models memorize training data and can leak personally identifiable information when queried with the right inputs, creating legal liability and privacy violations. Differential privacy adds calibrated noise to training or outputs to provide mathematical privacy guarantees. This is required for models trained on medical, financial, or user behavioral data under GDPR or HIPAA.

children.title: Red-Teaming & Safety Evaluations
children.description: LLMs and ML systems fail in adversarial ways that unit tests do not cover — prompt injection, jailbreaks, and out-of-distribution inputs that produce harmful outputs. Red-teaming systematically probes these failure modes before deployment. Without safety evaluations a model ships with known exploitable weaknesses that become public incidents.

data-scientist.title: Python for Data Science
data-scientist.description: Python is the language of data science. Get fluent in the core language and the notebook workflow you'll use daily.
data-scientist.challenge: Write a notebook that loads a CSV, computes summary stats, and answers 5 questions about it.

children.title: Python Syntax & Data Structures
children.description: Python's built-in types — lists, dicts, sets, tuples — and control flow are the primitives every data science library is built on. Shaky fundamentals mean you cannot read library source code, write helper functions, or debug unexpected behavior in notebooks.

children.title: Functions, Modules & Pythonic Idioms
children.description: Comprehensions, generators, and context managers reduce boilerplate and make data pipelines readable and memory-efficient. Engineers who miss these idioms write slow, verbose code that is painful to review and adapt.

children.title: Jupyter Notebooks Workflow
children.description: Notebooks are the primary medium for data exploration, reproducible analysis, and communicating findings. Not knowing how to structure cells, run magic commands, or export clean outputs slows every stage of a data science project.

children.title: Virtual Environments & pip
children.description: Isolated environments prevent dependency conflicts across projects and ensure reproducible installations. Without them, version mismatches silently corrupt analyses and make sharing projects with teammates unreliable.

resources.label: Real Python — pip and Virtual Environments

children.title: Reading & Writing Data Files
children.description: Data arrives as CSV, JSON, Excel, and plain text. Knowing how to read and write these formats correctly — handling encodings, delimiters, and schemas — is the entry point to every analysis and prevents silent data truncation.

resources.label: Real Python — Reading and Writing CSV Files

children.title: Python Data Science Handbook
children.description: Jake VanderPlas's open-access book covers NumPy, Pandas, Matplotlib, and Scikit-learn in depth with worked examples. It is one of the most-cited practical references in the data science community and aligns closely with day-to-day work.

children.title: Writing Readable Notebook Code
children.description: Notebooks become unreadable without naming conventions, markdown cells, and logical section order. Messy notebooks cannot be peer-reviewed, reproduced, or turned into reports — which defeats the purpose of the medium.

resources.label: Towards Data Science — Notebook Best Practices

children.title: Version Control with Git
children.description: Data science work involves constant iteration across experiments, datasets, and notebooks. Without Git, reverting a broken analysis or collaborating on a shared project becomes error-prone and stressful.

data-scientist.title: NumPy & Pandas
data-scientist.description: Master the two libraries at the heart of data work. Vectorize computations with NumPy and wrangle tables with Pandas.
data-scientist.challenge: Use Pandas to clean and aggregate a real dataset into a tidy summary table.

children.title: Arrays & Broadcasting
children.description: NumPy arrays replace Python loops with vectorized C operations that run orders of magnitude faster. Broadcasting rules allow operations on arrays of different shapes without copying data — misunderstanding them produces shape-mismatch errors or silently wrong results.

children.title: Vectorized Operations & Performance
children.description: Replacing explicit Python loops with NumPy ufuncs can give 100x speedups on numerical computations. Every data scientist who skips this eventually ships analysis code that times out on real-world dataset sizes.

children.title: Indexing, Filtering & Selection
children.description: loc vs iloc, boolean masks, and chained indexing are sources of silent copy-vs-view bugs in Pandas. Getting selection wrong produces SettingWithCopyWarnings that corrupt analysis results without raising errors.

resources.label: Pandas Docs — Indexing and Selecting Data
Real Python — Pandas loc vs iloc

children.title: GroupBy & Aggregation
children.description: GroupBy is the workhorse for summarizing data by category — computing means, counts, and custom aggregations per group. Misunderstanding the split-apply-combine pattern leads to incorrect business metrics that look plausible but are wrong.

children.title: Merge, Join & Concat
children.description: Real analyses combine multiple tables — orders with customers, events with users. Choosing the wrong join type (inner vs left vs outer) silently drops or duplicates rows, corrupting every downstream metric.

resources.label: Real Python — Pandas Merge, Join, and Concatenate

children.title: Tidy Data Principles
children.description: Tidy data — one observation per row, one variable per column — is what every visualization and modeling library expects. Violating these principles forces repeated reshaping that obscures the actual analysis logic.

resources.label: Towards Data Science — Tidy Data with Pandas

children.title: Memory Efficiency in Pandas
children.description: Loading a CSV with default dtypes can use 10x more memory than necessary. Downcasting integers and using categorical dtypes for low-cardinality columns is the difference between an analysis that fits in RAM and one that crashes.

resources.label: Pandas Docs — Scaling to Large Datasets
Real Python — Optimizing Pandas Memory Usage

children.title: Pandas vs NumPy: When to Use Each
children.description: Pandas adds labeled axes and heterogeneous column types on top of NumPy, but that overhead matters for tight numerical loops. Knowing when to drop to a raw NumPy array keeps data pipelines fast without sacrificing convenience.

resources.label: NumPy Docs — NumPy for Pandas Users
Towards Data Science — NumPy vs Pandas

data-scientist.title: Data Cleaning & Wrangling
data-scientist.description: Real data is messy. Learn to handle missing values, outliers and inconsistent formats to produce analysis-ready datasets.
data-scientist.challenge: Take a messy real-world dataset and turn it into a clean, documented, analysis-ready table.

children.title: Detecting & Imputing Missing Values
children.description: Missing data is rarely random — the pattern of missingness itself carries information. Blindly dropping rows deletes signal; imputing with the mean distorts distributions. Choosing the right strategy requires understanding why data is missing.

resources.label: Pandas Docs — Working with Missing Data
Towards Data Science — Handling Missing Data

children.title: Outlier Detection & Treatment
children.description: Outliers can be genuine extreme values, measurement errors, or fraud signals — treating them wrong corrupts statistical summaries and model training. IQR fences, z-scores, and isolation forests each suit different scenarios.

resources.label: Towards Data Science — Outlier Detection Methods

children.title: Type Conversion & Date Parsing
children.description: Pandas reads ambiguous columns as strings, silently preventing numeric aggregations and date arithmetic. Correct dtype assignment at load time prevents downstream errors in groupby operations, joins, and feature engineering.

resources.label: Pandas Docs — Working with Time Series
Real Python — Working with Dates and Times

children.title: String Cleaning & Normalization
children.description: Text columns in real data contain mixed casing, leading spaces, and inconsistent formatting that prevent accurate groupby and join operations. Pandas string methods and regex together handle the majority of these issues without custom loops.

resources.label: Pandas Docs — Working with Text Data

children.title: Reproducible ETL with Functions
children.description: Wrapping cleaning steps in named, tested functions rather than inline notebook cells makes the pipeline rerunnable and auditable. Ad-hoc notebook cleaning cannot be validated, versioned, or reused across datasets.

resources.label: Real Python — Refactoring Notebooks into Scripts

children.title: Combining Multiple Data Sources
children.description: Production datasets span databases, APIs, spreadsheets, and log files. Designing a merge strategy upfront prevents primary-key conflicts, fan-out row duplication, and the silent introduction of NULL rows that invalidate summary statistics.

resources.label: Towards Data Science — Data Wrangling with Pandas

children.title: Schema Validation & Assertions
children.description: Asserting expected row counts, column names, and value ranges catches upstream data changes before they silently corrupt an analysis. Without validation gates, bad data propagates through an entire pipeline and surfaces as wrong business decisions.

children.title: Deduplication Strategies
children.description: Duplicate records inflate counts, distort averages, and bias models toward over-represented entities. Exact deduplication with Pandas drop_duplicates and fuzzy deduplication with record linkage libraries catch different classes of the same problem.

resources.label: Towards Data Science — Fuzzy Matching in Python

data-scientist.title: EDA & Visualization
data-scientist.description: Understand your data before modeling. Explore distributions and relationships and communicate them with clear visuals.
data-scientist.challenge: Produce an EDA report with visuals that surfaces three non-obvious insights from a dataset.

children.title: Figure, Axes & the Object Model
children.description: Matplotlib's Figure/Axes hierarchy controls every visual element independently. Misunderstanding it causes layout bugs — overlapping labels, clipped titles, wrong subplot spacing — that make reports look unprofessional and are hard to fix without the mental model.

resources.label: Real Python — Python Plotting with Matplotlib

children.title: Choosing the Right Chart Type
children.description: Bar, line, scatter, histogram, and box plots each answer different questions. Using a pie chart for time series or a bar chart for distributions misleads the audience and can invert the conclusion a stakeholder draws from the data.

children.title: Distribution & Relationship Plots
children.description: Seaborn's histplot, kdeplot, and pairplot reveal the shape of data and pairwise relationships in one call. Skipping distributional checks leads to applying models with Gaussian assumptions to heavily skewed data, producing biased parameter estimates.

children.title: Categorical & Heatmap Plots
children.description: Box plots, violin plots, and heatmaps summarize grouped data and correlation matrices at a glance. These are the charts most frequently requested in stakeholder presentations and pull requests during code review of EDA notebooks.

children.title: Systematic EDA Process
children.description: A structured EDA — shape, dtypes, missing values, distributions, correlations, outliers — before modeling prevents wasted compute on fundamentally broken data. Teams that skip EDA consistently discover data issues only after model training, costing days of rework.

resources.label: Towards Data Science — EDA in Python

children.title: Correlation & Multicollinearity
children.description: High correlation between features misleads linear models into assigning arbitrary coefficients to collinear predictors. Spotting and removing multicollinear features during EDA prevents unstable coefficients and inflation of standard errors.

children.title: Plotly for Interactive Charts
children.description: Static charts lose information when exploring high-dimensional data. Plotly's hover tooltips, zoom, and filter controls let stakeholders self-serve answers — reducing the back-and-forth of repeated chart requests.

children.title: Publication-Ready Plot Styling
children.description: Default matplotlib styles look unpolished in reports and presentations. Consistent fonts, color palettes, and axis labels signal analytical credibility and ensure charts are readable in print and on projectors.

data-scientist.title: Statistics & Probability
data-scientist.description: Statistics is the foundation of sound conclusions. Learn the concepts that separate real signal from noise.
data-scientist.challenge: Run an A/B test analysis: state hypotheses, test for significance, and report the conclusion.

children.title: Central Tendency & Spread
children.description: Mean, median, variance, and standard deviation are the first lens on any dataset. Confusing mean and median on skewed distributions leads to reporting averages that no observation is close to — a credibility-destroying mistake in stakeholder reports.

children.title: Probability Distributions
children.description: Normal, binomial, Poisson, and exponential distributions describe different real-world phenomena. Choosing the wrong distribution family for a model assumption causes systematic prediction errors that are nearly impossible to diagnose without knowing the theory.

children.title: Hypothesis Testing & p-values
children.description: Hypothesis tests let you distinguish real effects from sampling noise. Misreading a p-value as the probability the null hypothesis is true — one of the most common errors in data science — leads to false claims and retracted conclusions.

children.title: Confidence Intervals
children.description: A confidence interval quantifies uncertainty around an estimate — something a point estimate alone cannot convey. Reporting results without confidence intervals hides sample-size dependencies and leads stakeholders to treat noisy estimates as precise facts.

children.title: Sampling Distributions & CLT
children.description: The Central Limit Theorem guarantees that sample means are approximately normal for large n regardless of the population distribution. Without this, you cannot justify t-tests, confidence intervals, or bootstrap methods on real-world datasets.

children.title: Bootstrap & Permutation Tests
children.description: When parametric assumptions fail, resampling methods provide distribution-free inference. Bootstrap confidence intervals and permutation tests are increasingly preferred in industry because they make fewer assumptions and are easier to explain to stakeholders.

children.title: Spurious Correlations & Confounders
children.description: Two variables can be perfectly correlated through a hidden third variable — ice cream sales and drowning rates both rise in summer. Confusing correlation with causation in business analysis drives policy decisions that backfire in production.

resources.label: Towards Data Science — Correlation vs Causation

children.title: A/B Testing Fundamentals
children.description: Randomized A/B tests are the gold standard for establishing causal effects in product decisions. Misdesigning a test — wrong sample size, peeking at results, or wrong metric — produces false positives that ship harmful product changes.

resources.label: Evan Miller — A/B Testing Sample Size Calculator

data-scientist.title: SQL & Data Sources
data-scientist.description: Most data lives in databases. Learn to extract and join it with SQL and pull from APIs.
data-scientist.challenge: Query a database with window functions and join the result with data pulled from an API.

children.title: SELECT, JOIN & GROUP BY
children.description: The vast majority of production data lives in relational databases. A data scientist who cannot write correct JOINs and GROUP BYs is blocked from self-service data access and dependent on engineering for every query, slowing every project.

children.title: Window Functions for Analytics
children.description: Window functions compute running totals, ranks, and period-over-period changes in a single SQL pass that GROUP BY cannot express. Teams that replace window functions with multiple subqueries produce slow, unmaintainable analytical queries.

children.title: Connecting Python to Databases
children.description: SQLAlchemy and psycopg2 let you query databases directly into Pandas DataFrames, eliminating CSV exports and manual data transfers. Without this, data pipelines require error-prone handoffs between systems.

children.title: Query Optimization Basics
children.description: Full table scans on large tables can take minutes when an indexed query takes milliseconds. Understanding EXPLAIN plans and index-friendly query patterns prevents analytical notebooks from timing out on production-scale data.

resources.label: Use The Index, Luke — SQL Indexing Guide

children.title: Consuming REST APIs with Python
children.description: Public and internal APIs are a primary source of real-time and supplementary data. Not knowing how to handle pagination, authentication, and rate limits blocks access to entire classes of datasets used in product analytics.

resources.label: Real Python — HTTP Requests with the requests Library

children.title: Handling Pagination & Rate Limits
children.description: Most APIs return data in pages of 100-1000 records and enforce per-minute request ceilings. Ignoring pagination truncates datasets; ignoring rate limits results in 429 errors that abort long-running data collection jobs.

resources.label: Real Python — Web Scraping with Python
Towards Data Science — Working with APIs in Python

children.title: Parquet & Columnar Formats
children.description: Parquet stores data column-by-column with built-in compression, making analytical queries 10-100x faster than CSV for wide tables. Teams that ship CSVs to production sacrifice both storage costs and query performance.

resources.label: Towards Data Science — Parquet vs CSV

children.title: Cloud Storage Basics (S3 / GCS)
children.description: Production data lives in object storage, not local filesystems. Reading from S3 or GCS with boto3 or fsspec is a prerequisite for working with large datasets in cloud-based analytics stacks.

resources.label: Real Python — Working with AWS S3

data-scientist.title: Machine Learning Foundations
data-scientist.description: Start predicting. Learn the core supervised learning algorithms with scikit-learn and how to apply them.
data-scientist.challenge: Train and compare three models on a dataset and report which performs best and why.

children.title: Linear & Logistic Regression
children.description: Linear regression is the baseline every other model must beat; logistic regression is the standard binary classifier. Understanding the coefficient interpretation and loss surface prevents teams from misapplying tree-based models to problems where linear models generalize better.

children.title: Decision Trees & Random Forests
children.description: Tree-based models require no feature scaling, handle missing values gracefully, and produce interpretable splits. Random forests reduce variance through ensemble averaging — misunderstanding max_depth and n_estimators produces either useless trees or overfit memorizers.

children.title: fit / predict / transform Pattern
children.description: Scikit-learn's consistent API — every estimator exposes fit, predict, and transform — lets you swap algorithms with one line. Not understanding this pattern blocks you from building pipelines and performing systematic model comparison.

children.title: Pipelines for Preprocessing + Modeling
children.description: sklearn Pipelines chain preprocessing and modeling steps so that cross-validation never leaks fitted scaler statistics from training into validation folds. This is the most common data leakage vector in data science code reviews.

children.title: Train / Test Split & Cross-Validation
children.description: Evaluating a model on its training data measures memorization, not generalization. k-fold cross-validation gives a more reliable performance estimate and is the minimum standard in any credible machine learning workflow.

children.title: Bias-Variance Tradeoff
children.description: Underfitting and overfitting are the two failure modes of every ML model. Understanding the bias-variance tradeoff tells you whether to add complexity, collect more data, or add regularization — without it, model improvement becomes trial and error.

children.title: K-Means Clustering
children.description: K-means is the first tool for customer segmentation and anomaly detection without labels. Choosing k incorrectly produces meaningless clusters — elbow plots and silhouette scores give principled guidance that prevents arbitrary segment counts in business reports.

children.title: Dimensionality Reduction with PCA
children.description: PCA compresses high-dimensional feature spaces while preserving maximum variance, enabling visualization and speeding up downstream models. Without PCA, models trained on hundreds of correlated features face the curse of dimensionality.

data-scientist.title: Model Evaluation & Features
data-scientist.description: Good models come from good features and honest evaluation. Learn to measure, tune and improve performance.
data-scientist.challenge: Improve a baseline model with feature engineering and tuning, documenting the metric gains.

children.title: Precision, Recall, F1 & ROC-AUC
children.description: Accuracy is misleading on imbalanced datasets — a model predicting the majority class always scores high. Precision-recall tradeoffs determine real-world cost of false positives vs false negatives, and choosing the wrong metric drives the model toward the wrong behavior.

children.title: Regression Metrics: MAE, RMSE, R2
children.description: RMSE penalizes large errors quadratically while MAE treats all errors equally — the choice should match the business cost of errors. Using R2 without checking residuals hides systematic prediction failures in specific subgroups.

resources.label: Towards Data Science — MAE vs RMSE

children.title: Encoding Categorical Variables
children.description: Linear models and neural networks require numeric input; tree models have their own requirements. One-hot encoding high-cardinality features explodes feature spaces; target encoding leaks if not applied inside cross-validation folds.

children.title: Scaling & Normalization
children.description: Distance-based models and gradient-based optimizers are highly sensitive to feature magnitude. Fitting a scaler on the entire dataset — including validation data — is data leakage that inflates evaluation scores and degrades production performance.

children.title: GridSearchCV & RandomizedSearchCV
children.description: Manually tuning hyperparameters is inefficient and non-reproducible. Grid search exhaustively tests combinations; randomized search finds good regions faster in high-dimensional hyperparameter spaces — both must be applied with cross-validation to avoid overfitting the validation set.

children.title: Avoiding Data Leakage
children.description: Data leakage — when information from the future or the test set influences training — is the most common cause of impressive benchmark scores that collapse in production. Every preprocessing step must happen inside the cross-validation loop.

children.title: Resampling: SMOTE & Undersampling
children.description: Class imbalance — common in fraud, churn, and medical datasets — causes models to predict the majority class for everything. SMOTE generates synthetic minority examples; undersampling removes majority examples; both change the decision boundary in measurable ways.

resources.label: Towards Data Science — Handling Imbalanced Data

children.title: Class Weights & Threshold Tuning
children.description: Adjusting class weights in the loss function is often more stable than resampling and requires no data modification. Shifting the classification threshold after training lets you trade precision for recall without retraining — critical in production cost-sensitive systems.

data-scientist.title: Communicating Insights
data-scientist.description: An insight no one understands has no value. Learn to communicate findings to stakeholders clearly.
data-scientist.challenge: Turn an analysis into a Streamlit dashboard and a 5-slide narrative for stakeholders.

children.title: Structuring a Data Narrative
children.description: Stakeholders make decisions from stories, not tables. A narrative that leads with the business question, presents evidence visually, and ends with a clear recommendation converts correct analysis into action — without this structure, correct findings are routinely ignored.

children.title: Choosing Metrics That Matter
children.description: North Star metrics focus teams; vanity metrics inflate confidence without guiding decisions. Misaligning your analysis metric with the business outcome — such as optimizing click rate when revenue per session is what matters — drives optimization in the wrong direction.

resources.label: Towards Data Science — Picking the Right Metric
Lenny's Newsletter — North Star Metric Guide

children.title: Clean Notebook Reports with nbconvert
children.description: Notebooks must be reproducible — running top to bottom with a fresh kernel should reproduce all outputs. nbconvert exports polished HTML and PDF reports that non-technical stakeholders can read without Jupyter installed.

children.title: Quarto & Literate Programming
children.description: Quarto renders notebooks into presentation slides, HTML reports, and PDFs from a single source file. Teams that adopt literate programming spend less time reformatting the same analysis for different audiences.

children.title: Building Dashboards with Streamlit
children.description: Streamlit turns a Python script into an interactive web dashboard in minutes — no front-end experience required. Stakeholders self-serve answers from dashboards rather than requesting one-off analyses, multiplying the impact of a single data scientist.

children.title: Tableau & Business Intelligence Tools
children.description: Many organizations standardize on Tableau, Power BI, or Looker for executive dashboards. A data scientist who cannot publish to these platforms creates a last-mile delivery gap that prevents findings from reaching decision-makers.

resources.label: Towards Data Science — Tableau for Data Scientists

children.title: Simplifying Technical Findings
children.description: A p-value means nothing to a VP of Marketing. Translating model outputs — confidence intervals, feature importances, error rates — into plain language action items is what separates impactful analysts from those whose work sits unread in Confluence.

resources.label: Harvard Business Review — Presenting Data to Executives

children.title: Slide Design for Data Presentations
children.description: One chart per slide with a descriptive headline that states the conclusion — not the topic — is the most consistently effective data presentation format. Slide decks that bury the insight in a title like 'Revenue by Quarter' require the audience to do the analyst's job.

resources.label: Cole Nussbaumer Knaflic — Storytelling with Data Book

data-scientist.title: Capstone & Deployment
data-scientist.description: Bring it together. Run a full project from question to deployed model and a reproducible pipeline.
data-scientist.challenge: Ship an end-to-end project: clean data, train a model, track it, and serve predictions via an API.

children.title: Framing the Data Science Problem
children.description: Translating a business question into a concrete ML task — what to predict, with what features, evaluated by what metric — is harder than the modeling itself. Misframing the problem means a perfectly trained model solves the wrong thing and creates no business value.

resources.label: Towards Data Science — Framing ML Problems

children.title: Reproducible Project Structure
children.description: Cookiecutter Data Science provides a standardized directory layout that makes projects navigable for collaborators and reproducible across machines. Ad-hoc folder structures cause rework when onboarding teammates or resuming a project after weeks away.

children.title: Logging Parameters, Metrics & Artifacts
children.description: Without experiment tracking, the best configuration from 50 runs exists only in memory. MLflow records every hyperparameter, metric, and model artifact so you can reproduce any past experiment and compare runs systematically.

children.title: MLflow Model Registry
children.description: The MLflow Model Registry provides versioned model storage with staging, production, and archived lifecycle states. Without a registry, promoting a model to production is an undocumented, irreversible manual step prone to deploying the wrong artifact.

resources.label: Towards Data Science — MLflow in Practice

children.title: Building a Prediction Endpoint
children.description: A model that runs only in a notebook delivers zero value to users. FastAPI exposes predictions as a REST endpoint in under 50 lines and validates inputs with Pydantic, catching malformed requests before they reach the model and produce silent wrong predictions.

children.title: Loading & Caching ML Models in FastAPI
children.description: Loading a scikit-learn or XGBoost model from disk on every request adds hundreds of milliseconds of latency. Caching the model at startup with a startup event handler keeps inference fast and avoids resource exhaustion under concurrent load.

resources.label: FastAPI Docs — Startup and Shutdown Events
Towards Data Science — Deploy ML Model with FastAPI

children.title: Environment Reproducibility
children.description: Pinning dependencies in requirements.txt or environment.yml ensures that a project runs identically on any machine. Without pinning, library updates silently break models — a problem that surfaces only when a colleague tries to reproduce your results.

children.title: Sharing Projects on GitHub & Kaggle
children.description: A public GitHub repository with a documented README and Kaggle notebook portfolio is the de facto data science portfolio. Employers and collaborators evaluate technical depth from open-source contributions — a private codebase is invisible to the community.

ai-developer.title: Python & APIs for AI
ai-developer.description: AI developers glue models to applications. Get comfortable with Python and consuming HTTP APIs.
ai-developer.challenge: Build a Python script that calls an LLM API and handles errors, retries and token limits.

children.title: Python Essentials for AI
children.description: Python is the lingua franca of AI development. Without a solid grasp of list comprehensions, generators, and async patterns, your integration code will be brittle and slow under real API load.

children.title: Environment & Secrets Management
children.description: Leaking API keys is a critical security failure. Using .env files and a library like python-dotenv keeps secrets out of version control and makes credentials swappable across environments without code changes.

children.title: Consuming REST APIs with httpx
children.description: AI services are exposed over HTTP. Understanding request/response cycles, headers, and async HTTP with httpx lets you call multiple model endpoints concurrently without blocking your application.

children.title: Rate Limits & Retry Logic
children.description: LLM APIs enforce per-minute token and request limits. Without exponential backoff and retry logic, bursts of requests will surface as hard failures that crash your app in production.

children.title: Working with JSON in Python
children.description: Almost every AI API response is JSON. Knowing how to parse, validate, and serialize nested structures cleanly is the baseline skill for passing data between your app and any model endpoint.

children.title: Tokens & Cost Awareness
children.description: LLM pricing is per-token, not per-request. Misunderstanding tokenization leads to blown budgets and context-window overflows that silently truncate your prompts mid-sentence.

children.title: Error Handling & Status Codes
children.description: APIs return 429, 500, and 503 codes regularly in production. Distinguishing retriable transient errors from permanent failures determines whether your app degrades gracefully or crashes completely.

children.title: Structuring AI Project Code
children.description: Keeping prompt templates, API clients, and business logic in separate modules prevents spaghetti code as your app grows. A clean structure makes prompts testable and model-swapping low risk.

ai-developer.title: Working with LLM APIs
ai-developer.description: Learn the building blocks of LLM apps. Use chat completions, system prompts and streaming responses.
ai-developer.challenge: Build a streaming chat assistant with a custom system prompt and adjustable parameters.

children.title: Roles: System, User & Assistant
children.description: The three-role message structure is the contract between your app and the model. Misusing roles — e.g., putting instructions in the user turn — weakens model instruction-following and makes jailbreaks easier.

children.title: Temperature & Sampling Parameters
children.description: Temperature, top-p, and max-tokens control the creativity-vs-determinism tradeoff. Setting these incorrectly causes unpredictable outputs in production or token-budget overruns that truncate responses.

children.title: Handling Context Windows
children.description: Every model has a fixed token budget shared by input and output. Exceeding it silently truncates older messages, causing the model to lose conversation history and give incoherent replies.

children.title: Multimodal Inputs
children.description: Modern LLMs accept images, audio, and files alongside text. Understanding how each modality consumes tokens and what formats are accepted prevents silent failures when passing non-text content.

children.title: Server-Sent Events & Streaming
children.description: LLMs generate tokens sequentially. Without streaming, users stare at a blank screen for seconds. Implementing SSE-based streaming dramatically improves perceived responsiveness in chat applications.

children.title: Building a Chat Loop
children.description: A chat application must maintain an accumulating message history and send it with every request. Breaking this loop — forgetting messages or ordering them incorrectly — destroys coherent conversation.

children.title: Choosing the Right Model
children.description: Picking a model is a latency-cost-quality tradeoff. Using a flagship model for every task wastes money; using a weak model for complex reasoning yields bad outputs that damage user trust.

children.title: Provider Fallback Strategies
children.description: No LLM provider guarantees 100% uptime. Building fallback logic — trying a secondary provider or model when the primary fails — is the difference between a resilient product and one that goes dark during incidents.

ai-developer.title: Prompt Engineering for Developers
ai-developer.description: Get reliable results from models. Apply prompting techniques that make outputs accurate and parseable.
ai-developer.challenge: Design prompts that reliably return valid JSON for a structured extraction task.

children.title: Zero-shot vs Few-shot Prompting
children.description: Few-shot examples are the fastest way to shift model behavior without fine-tuning. Omitting examples when the task is novel or ambiguous leads to inconsistent output formats that break downstream parsing.

children.title: Chain-of-Thought Reasoning
children.description: Asking the model to reason step-by-step before answering measurably improves accuracy on multi-step problems. Skipping it on logic or math tasks causes the model to jump to wrong answers confidently.

children.title: JSON Mode & Structured Outputs
children.description: Returning free-form text when downstream code expects JSON causes silent parsing failures. JSON mode and structured output schemas force the model to emit valid, schema-conformant data every time.

children.title: Reducing Hallucination
children.description: Models confidently fabricate facts when context is insufficient. Grounding prompts in retrieved documents, asking for uncertainty acknowledgment, and using low temperature reduce hallucination rates significantly.

children.title: Function Calling Basics
children.description: Function calling lets the model signal which tool to invoke and with what arguments. Without understanding how to define tool schemas, the model either ignores tools or produces malformed argument objects.

children.title: Prompt Templates
children.description: Hard-coding prompts inside application logic makes them untestable and version-uncontrolled. Storing prompt templates as separate, parameterized strings enables A/B testing and safe iteration without touching business logic.

children.title: Prompt Injection Defense
children.description: User-supplied input embedded directly into system prompts can hijack model behavior. Sanitizing inputs and separating system instructions from user data is the foundational defense against prompt injection attacks.

children.title: Iterative Prompt Evaluation
children.description: Prompts that work in a notebook often fail on edge-case inputs in production. Building a small golden-set of test inputs and scoring prompt changes against it prevents regressions as the product evolves.

ai-developer.title: LangChain Fundamentals
ai-developer.description: Compose LLM applications with LangChain. Build chains, manage prompts and add memory.
ai-developer.challenge: Build a multi-step chain that summarizes a document and answers follow-up questions with memory.

children.title: LCEL — LangChain Expression Language
children.description: LCEL is the composition layer that connects prompts, models, and output parsers into pipelines using the pipe operator. Ignoring LCEL forces you to write fragile imperative glue code that breaks when chain steps change.

children.title: Output Parsers
