rl-engineer.challenge: Solve a gridworld with both policy iteration and Monte Carlo control and compare them.

children.title: Policy Iteration
children.description: Policy iteration alternates between exact policy evaluation and greedy improvement and is guaranteed to converge to the optimal policy. Understanding why it converges explains the mechanics behind every approximate actor-critic algorithm.

children.title: Value Iteration
children.description: Value iteration collapses policy evaluation and improvement into a single Bellman update, converging faster in practice. Knowing when to prefer value iteration over policy iteration prevents unnecessary computation in planning problems.

children.title: Monte Carlo Prediction
children.description: Monte Carlo estimates value by averaging complete-episode returns rather than bootstrapping. Ignoring its high variance relative to TD methods causes slow convergence and instability when episode lengths are long.

children.title: Monte Carlo Control
children.description: MC control learns an optimal policy without a model of the environment by interleaving policy improvement with episode-based estimation. Misapplying it to continuing tasks breaks the return calculation entirely.

resources.label: Hugging Face Deep RL — MC vs TD

children.title: High Variance in Monte Carlo
children.description: Because MC uses full returns, a single bad trajectory can dominate the estimate. Failing to address variance — through baselines or lower-variance estimators — makes training erratic and sample-inefficient.

resources.label: Spinning Up — Intro to Policy Optimization

children.title: Bias from Bootstrapping in DP
children.description: DP and TD methods bootstrap from value estimates that start as random guesses, introducing bias that propagates through updates. Understanding this bias-variance spectrum is the foundation for choosing TD(lambda) and GAE parameters correctly.

children.title: Exploring Starts
children.description: Exploring starts guarantees coverage of all state-action pairs by randomising episode initialisation. Without it, Monte Carlo control can converge to a suboptimal policy that never visits important states.

children.title: Epsilon-Greedy for Control
children.description: Epsilon-greedy is the practical alternative to exploring starts for environments where episode initialisation cannot be controlled. Setting epsilon too high or decaying it too slowly wastes samples on worthless exploration after early learning.

rl-engineer.title: Temporal-Difference & Q-Learning
rl-engineer.description: TD learning combines the best of DP and Monte Carlo. Build tabular Q-learning agents.
rl-engineer.challenge: Implement Q-learning from scratch and train it to solve the Taxi or FrozenLake environment.

children.title: TD(0) Prediction
children.description: TD(0) updates value estimates after every step using a bootstrapped target, combining MC's model-free nature with DP's step-level updates. Skipping this baseline makes it hard to debug whether failures in Q-learning come from the update rule or the exploration policy.

children.title: SARSA — On-Policy TD Control
children.description: SARSA updates Q values using the action actually taken by the current policy, making it safe for environments with dangerous states. Mistaking it for off-policy creates agents that ignore risk because they train on greedy actions they never actually execute.

children.title: The Q-Learning Update Rule
children.description: Q-learning is off-policy because it always bootstraps from the max Q value regardless of the exploration policy used. Misunderstanding this causes incorrect credit assignment when the behaviour policy differs significantly from greedy.

children.title: Tabular Limitations and Scalability
children.description: Tabular Q-learning stores one value per state-action pair, making it infeasible beyond small discrete spaces. Recognising this bottleneck early motivates function approximation and prepares you to understand why DQN was necessary.

resources.label: Lilian Weng — A (Long) Peek into RL

children.title: Learning Rate Scheduling
children.description: TD convergence proofs require the learning rate to satisfy Robbins-Monro conditions. Using a fixed learning rate that is too large causes oscillation; too small and the agent never learns within a practical budget.

children.title: Deadly Triad in TD with Function Approximation
children.description: Combining TD, function approximation, and off-policy data creates the deadly triad that can cause divergence. Awareness of this interaction is the first line of defence when debugging unstable deep RL training runs.

children.title: Epsilon Decay Schedules
children.description: Epsilon must be decayed over training so the agent shifts from exploration to exploitation. A poorly tuned decay schedule is one of the most common reasons a working Q-learning implementation fails to reach peak performance.

resources.label: Hugging Face Deep RL — Exploration Strategies

children.title: Optimistic Initialisation
children.description: Initialising Q values above the expected reward range forces early exploration of all actions before exploitation. This simple trick eliminates missed state coverage bugs without the complexity of count-based exploration methods.

rl-engineer.title: Deep Q-Networks
rl-engineer.description: Scale RL with neural networks. Build DQN agents that learn from high-dimensional states.
rl-engineer.challenge: Build a DQN in PyTorch with replay and target networks to play a classic control environment.

children.title: Experience Replay Buffer
children.description: Replay breaks temporal correlations between consecutive transitions, which would otherwise cause the neural network to overfit recent experience and forget earlier states. Without a properly sized buffer, DQN training is equivalent to online gradient descent with correlated data.

resources.label: Lilian Weng — From DQN to Rainbow

children.title: Target Networks
children.description: A slowly updated target network provides stable regression targets during training. Without it, the Q-network chases a moving target that it is simultaneously updating, a feedback loop that causes divergence in most non-trivial environments.

children.title: Double DQN
children.description: Standard DQN overestimates Q values because it uses the same network to select and evaluate actions. Double DQN decouples these steps to reduce overestimation bias, directly improving policy quality on most benchmarks.

resources.label: Lilian Weng — From DQN to Rainbow

children.title: Dueling Network Architecture
children.description: Separating the value stream from the advantage stream lets the network learn state values independently of actions, improving learning efficiency especially in states where action choice barely matters.

resources.label: Lilian Weng — From DQN to Rainbow

children.title: Neural Network as Q-Function
children.description: The Q-network maps states to Q values for all actions in a single forward pass. Incorrect output head sizing — outputting a scalar instead of a vector over actions — is a silent bug that produces training loss values that look plausible but represent nothing meaningful.

children.title: Gradient Clipping for Stability
children.description: RL loss surfaces are non-stationary and can produce explosive gradients. Gradient clipping is a standard stabiliser in DQN implementations; omitting it on hard environments results in NaN weights that are easily mistaken for a reward shaping problem.

children.title: Monitoring Q-Value Magnitude
children.description: Tracking average Q values during training is the fastest way to detect overestimation and divergence before they collapse agent performance. Ignoring these diagnostics means you find out about instability only after wasting hours of compute.

children.title: Replay Buffer Warm-Up
children.description: Training before the buffer has accumulated sufficient diverse transitions causes early overfitting to a narrow distribution. A warm-up period that fills the buffer with random-policy data before gradient updates is standard practice to avoid this failure mode.

rl-engineer.title: Policy Gradient Methods
rl-engineer.description: Optimize policies directly. Learn policy gradient methods for continuous and stochastic control.
rl-engineer.challenge: Implement REINFORCE with a baseline and train it on a continuous control task.

children.title: Policy Parameterization
children.description: Policy gradient methods represent the policy as a differentiable function of parameters, enabling direct optimisation via gradient ascent on expected return. Using a non-differentiable policy class or forgetting to apply softmax to logits makes the gradient undefined and training silent fails.

resources.label: Spinning Up — Intro to Policy Optimization

children.title: Log-Probability Gradient Trick
children.description: The score function estimator rewrites the gradient of expected return as an expectation, making it computable from samples. Misunderstanding the derivation causes incorrect implementation of the loss — a common bug is maximising log probability without weighting by the return.

children.title: Monte Carlo Policy Gradient
children.description: REINFORCE uses full-episode returns to weight log probabilities, producing an unbiased but high-variance gradient estimate. The high variance means training curves often look flat for many episodes before suddenly improving, which is frequently misdiagnosed as a bug.

resources.label: Hugging Face Deep RL — Policy Gradient

children.title: Return Normalisation
children.description: Normalising returns within a batch reduces gradient variance and stabilises early training. Skipping normalisation causes the gradient scale to vary wildly across episodes, making learning rate selection extremely sensitive.

children.title: Baselines
children.description: Subtracting a baseline from the return does not bias the gradient but can dramatically reduce its variance. The most common baseline is the state value V(s); omitting it roughly doubles the number of samples needed for convergence.

children.title: Entropy Regularisation
children.description: Adding an entropy bonus to the objective encourages the policy to remain stochastic and exploratory throughout training. Without it, policy gradient agents often collapse to a suboptimal deterministic policy early in training and cannot recover.

resources.label: Spinning Up — Intro to Policy Optimization

children.title: Gaussian Policy for Continuous Control
children.description: Continuous actions require a parametric distribution such as a Gaussian over action space. Outputting raw unbounded values instead of distribution parameters makes the action space effectively discrete and prevents learning in locomotion tasks.

children.title: Action Clipping vs Squashing
children.description: Hard clipping gradients through action bounds breaks the gradient flow for policy improvement. Using tanh squashing with log-probability correction preserves the gradient and is the standard solution for bounded continuous action spaces.

rl-engineer.title: Actor-Critic & PPO
rl-engineer.description: Combine value and policy learning. Master the actor-critic family and PPO, the modern workhorse.
rl-engineer.challenge: Train a PPO agent to solve a continuous control environment and tune it to a target reward.

children.title: Shared vs Separate Networks
children.description: The actor and critic can share a backbone or use entirely separate networks. Sharing parameters can speed learning through feature reuse but creates conflicting gradients; choosing wrong for the task leads to training instability that is hard to distinguish from a bad learning rate.

children.title: Generalised Advantage Estimation (GAE)
children.description: GAE interpolates between high-variance MC returns and biased one-step TD errors via the lambda parameter, giving practitioners fine-grained control over the bias-variance trade-off in advantage estimation. Ignoring GAE forces you to choose between slow and noisy training.

children.title: Clipped Surrogate Objective
children.description: PPO clips the policy ratio to prevent large destructive updates that collapse performance. Without clipping, gradient steps can shift the policy so far that the agent enters a bad region of policy space it cannot recover from in reasonable time.

children.title: Multiple Epochs on Collected Data
children.description: PPO reuses each batch of collected experience for multiple gradient updates, dramatically improving sample efficiency over vanilla policy gradient. Using too many epochs violates the importance weighting assumption and causes policy collapse.

children.title: Synchronous Parallel Rollouts (A2C)
children.description: A2C runs multiple environment workers in parallel and averages their gradients synchronously, reducing variance without asynchronous communication overhead. Omitting parallelism makes A2C equivalent to a single-worker policy gradient with no variance benefit.

children.title: Advantage Normalisation
children.description: Normalising advantages across the mini-batch during each PPO epoch keeps gradient magnitudes consistent as the policy updates. Skipping it means early mini-batches in an epoch use correct-scale gradients while later ones receive inflated or deflated updates.

children.title: Clip Ratio and KL Divergence
children.description: The clip ratio epsilon directly controls policy update magnitude. Setting it too high reverts to unconstrained policy gradient; too low and learning stalls because updates are negligible — monitoring KL divergence is the standard diagnostic.

children.title: Value Function Loss Coefficient
children.description: The coefficient balancing actor and critic losses determines which signal dominates shared-network training. An untuned coefficient causes the shared representation to optimise for the wrong objective, degrading both actor and critic quality.

rl-engineer.title: Environments with Gymnasium
rl-engineer.description: Standardize your RL workflow. Use Gymnasium environments and build custom ones for your problems.
rl-engineer.challenge: Build a custom Gymnasium environment for a toy problem and train an agent to solve it.

children.title: reset() and step() Contract
children.description: Every Gymnasium environment exposes reset() returning an initial observation and step() returning (obs, reward, terminated, truncated, info). Breaking this contract — for example returning wrong array shapes — causes silent data corruption in replay buffers and value normalisation.

children.title: Observation and Action Spaces
children.description: Gymnasium spaces define the dtype and shape of observations and actions. Mismatch between the declared space and actual data causes runtime errors in wrappers and algorithm code that are frequently misattributed to model architecture choices.

children.title: Observation and Reward Wrappers
children.description: Wrappers compose preprocessing steps — normalising observations, clipping rewards, stacking frames — without modifying the base environment. Implementing preprocessing inside the agent instead of as wrappers makes it incompatible with vectorised environments and baseline libraries.

children.title: FrameStack and TimeLimit Wrappers
children.description: FrameStack provides temporal context that satisfies the Markov property for visual environments; TimeLimit enforces episode truncation for continuing tasks. Missing either wrapper on the appropriate environment causes systematic performance degradation that looks like a learning algorithm problem.

children.title: SyncVectorEnv and AsyncVectorEnv
children.description: Vectorised environments step multiple environment instances in parallel and batch their outputs, multiplying data collection throughput without changing the algorithm. Forgetting to account for auto-reset semantics in vectorised environments corrupts the first observation of each new episode.

children.title: Stable-Baselines3 VecEnv Integration
children.description: SB3 provides its own VecEnv abstraction that normalises rewards and observations across workers. Using raw Gymnasium VecEnv with SB3 algorithms skips these normalisations and typically results in 10x slower convergence on continuous control benchmarks.

children.title: Reward Shaping in Custom Envs
children.description: Reward shaping guides the agent toward desired behaviour when sparse rewards are impractical. Poorly shaped rewards are the most common reason custom environments fail to produce useful agents — they teach the agent to exploit the shaping rather than solve the task.

children.title: Validating Environments with check_env
children.description: Stable-Baselines3 provides check_env() to verify observation shapes, space declarations, and step return formats. Skipping this validation before training leads to cryptic shape mismatch errors hours into a training run.

rl-engineer.title: Scaling RL with Ray RLlib
rl-engineer.description: Train serious RL at scale. Use Ray RLlib to run distributed training across many workers.
rl-engineer.challenge: Train a PPO agent with RLlib across multiple workers and tune it with Ray Tune.

children.title: Ray Remote Functions and Actors
children.description: Ray parallelises Python code via remote functions (stateless) and actors (stateful). Treating Ray actors as stateless or failing to handle actor failures causes silent data loss in distributed rollout workers that is very difficult to reproduce locally.

children.title: Object Store and Shared Memory
children.description: Ray moves large objects through a shared-memory plasma store rather than serialising them through the driver. Passing large NumPy arrays as Python arguments instead of Ray object references causes serialisation bottlenecks that eliminate the parallelism benefit.

children.title: AlgorithmConfig API
children.description: RLlib 2.x uses a typed AlgorithmConfig builder to define training configuration rather than plain dictionaries. Using the deprecated dict-based config causes silent parameter silencing where unrecognised keys are ignored and the algorithm trains with wrong settings.

children.title: Distributed Rollout Workers
children.description: RLlib spawns Ray actor workers that collect experience in parallel and send batches to the learner. Misconfiguring num_rollout_workers or not accounting for CPU vs GPU resource allocation starves workers and results in a GPU that sits idle while CPUs bottleneck.

children.title: Hyperparameter Search with Tune
children.description: Ray Tune automates hyperparameter search using schedulers like ASHA that early-stop bad trials. Running manual grid search instead of Tune on RL problems wastes 10x more compute because RL metrics are noisy and require principled early stopping.

children.title: Checkpointing and Fault Tolerance
children.description: Long RL training runs on clusters are prone to node failures; RLlib supports periodic checkpointing and trial resumption via Tune. Skipping checkpoints means a single worker crash after hours of training loses all progress.

children.title: Autoscaling with Ray Clusters
children.description: Ray's autoscaler adds and removes nodes based on resource demand, enabling cost-efficient distributed training on cloud providers. Without autoscaling, either idle nodes waste money or insufficient nodes become the training bottleneck.

children.title: Monitoring with Ray Dashboard
children.description: The Ray Dashboard exposes per-actor CPU/GPU utilisation, task durations, and object store usage in real time. Without it, diagnosing whether a training bottleneck is the learner, the rollout workers, or network I/O requires guesswork.

rl-engineer.title: Applications & Deployment
rl-engineer.description: Take RL from notebook to product. Learn real-world applications and how to deploy trained policies.
rl-engineer.challenge: Export a trained policy and serve it behind an API that returns actions for given observations.

children.title: Offline RL and Distribution Shift
children.description: Offline RL trains entirely on a fixed dataset without environment interaction, causing out-of-distribution generalisation errors when the policy visits state-action pairs underrepresented in the data. Ignoring distribution shift leads to overconfident Q estimates and catastrophic policy failures at deployment.

children.title: Sim-to-Real Transfer
children.description: The simulation-to-real gap arises from differences in physics, sensor noise, and latency. Domain randomisation during training is the primary tool to close this gap; without it, policies that score perfectly in simulation often fail completely on physical hardware.

children.title: Exporting Policies with Ray Serve
children.description: Ray Serve deploys trained RLlib policies as HTTP endpoints that return actions for incoming observations. Forgetting to normalise inference-time observations with the same statistics used during training causes the policy to receive out-of-distribution inputs and produce degraded actions.

children.title: TorchScript for Portable Inference
children.description: Tracing a PyTorch policy with TorchScript removes the Python runtime dependency, enabling deployment in C++ or mobile environments. Untraceable dynamic control flow in the policy network is the most common obstacle and must be resolved before production deployment.

children.title: Constrained RL and Safe Exploration
children.description: Real deployments must respect safety constraints such as force limits in robotics or budget limits in recommendation systems. Ignoring constraint satisfaction during training produces policies that maximise reward at the cost of real-world failures that cannot be corrected after deployment.

children.title: Monitoring Deployed Agents
children.description: Deployed policies experience distribution shift as the real environment evolves; monitoring action distributions and reward signals in production is the only way to detect silent performance degradation before it causes user-facing failures.

children.title: Recommendation Systems with RL
children.description: Recommendation is a high-dimensional contextual bandit or full MDP where the reward is user engagement. Modelling it as a supervised problem ignores the sequential nature of user sessions and leads to policies that optimise click-through rate at the expense of long-term user satisfaction.

children.title: RLHF in Large Language Models
children.description: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback fine-tunes LLMs with PPO against a learned reward model trained on human preferences. Understanding standard RL concepts — policy, reward, KL penalty — is a prerequisite to debugging RLHF training instabilities.

prompt-engineer.title: LLM & Prompting Foundations
prompt-engineer.description: Effective prompting starts with understanding how models work. Build the mental model behind every good prompt.
prompt-engineer.challenge: Take one task and show how temperature and phrasing change the model's output quality.

children.title: Token Prediction & Context Windows
children.description: LLMs generate text one token at a time, predicting the next token based on all prior context. If you treat the model as a black box you will never understand why long prompts degrade, why order matters, or why certain phrasings reliably fail.

resources.label: Anthropic — Core Views on AI Safety

children.title: Temperature, Sampling & Hallucination
children.description: Temperature controls randomness in token selection; too high and the model drifts, too low and it becomes repetitive. Understanding hallucination as a confidence-mismatch problem lets you write prompts that surface uncertainty rather than hide it.

children.title: System vs User vs Assistant Roles
children.description: The role structure is not cosmetic — models weight system instructions differently from user turns. Misplacing critical constraints in the wrong role is one of the most common reasons prompts silently fail in production.

children.title: Prompt Components & Their Purpose
children.description: A prompt is a composition of task description, context, examples, output format, and constraints. Knowing which component is responsible for which output property lets you surgically fix failures instead of rewriting prompts from scratch.

children.title: Open vs Closed Models
children.description: Closed API models (GPT-4o, Claude) offer ease and capability; open models (Llama, Mistral) offer control and cost savings. Choosing the wrong tier for a task is expensive — prompting strategy must account for the model's instruction-following strength.

children.title: Context Window Constraints
children.description: Every model has a hard token limit for input plus output combined. Running over it silently truncates context, causing the model to ignore instructions or retrieved data that appeared early. Managing token budget is a first-class prompting concern.

children.title: Iterative Prompt Refinement
children.description: Good prompts are written in cycles, not in one shot. Starting with a minimal prompt and adding specificity only where outputs fail produces cleaner, more maintainable prompts than writing long instructions upfront.

children.title: Playground & Experiment Mindset
children.description: Treating prompting as an empirical discipline — forming a hypothesis, testing, measuring — separates engineers who improve reliably from those who guess. Every major AI provider offers a playground; use it as your lab before writing a line of code.

prompt-engineer.title: Core Prompting Techniques
prompt-engineer.description: Learn the techniques that reliably improve outputs. These are the bread and butter of prompt engineering.
prompt-engineer.challenge: Improve a weak prompt through five iterations, documenting the gain at each step.

children.title: Zero-shot Instruction Clarity
children.description: Zero-shot prompting lives or dies on instruction clarity — vague verbs like 'summarize' or 'analyze' leave the model to guess your intent. Being explicit about audience, length, tone, and format eliminates the most common source of zero-shot failures.

children.title: Few-shot Example Selection
children.description: Few-shot examples teach the model format, style, and reasoning pattern simultaneously. Poorly chosen examples — wrong distribution, inconsistent format, or incorrect labels — actively hurt performance by teaching the wrong pattern.

children.title: Assigning Expert Roles
children.description: Telling the model 'you are a senior security engineer' shifts its prior toward domain vocabulary, cautious phrasing, and structured analysis. Without a role, the model defaults to a generalist assistant voice that may be too hedged for technical tasks.

children.title: Personas vs Personas with Constraints
children.description: A persona without constraints can drift — the model adopts a character but still hallucinates or breaks format. Combining a role with explicit output rules produces the most reliable steering, because the persona sets tone while constraints control structure.

children.title: Structural Delimiters
children.description: Delimiters like triple backticks, XML tags, or section headers prevent the model from confusing user data with instructions — a critical defense against accidental prompt injection. Their absence is a common cause of unpredictable outputs when input contains special characters.

children.title: Output Format Specification
children.description: Specifying output format — markdown, numbered list, JSON skeleton — dramatically reduces post-processing work and prevents the model from choosing a structure that breaks downstream code. Format instructions belong at both the start and end of long prompts for maximum reliability.

children.title: Systematic Prompt Iteration
children.description: Changing multiple things between prompt versions makes it impossible to know what caused improvement or regression. Disciplined one-change-at-a-time iteration with a consistent test set is what separates engineering from guesswork.

children.title: Failure Mode Analysis
children.description: Categorizing prompt failures — wrong format, hallucinated content, instruction ignored, off-topic — points directly at which prompt component to fix. Treating all failures as 'bad output' and rewriting from scratch is the single biggest time sink in prompt work.

prompt-engineer.title: Advanced Prompting
prompt-engineer.description: Unlock reasoning. Apply advanced techniques that help models tackle complex, multi-step problems.
prompt-engineer.challenge: Use chain-of-thought and self-consistency to boost accuracy on a reasoning task.

children.title: Why CoT Works
children.description: Chain-of-thought prompting forces the model to externalize intermediate reasoning steps before committing to an answer, which dramatically reduces errors on multi-step arithmetic, logic, and planning tasks. Without it, the model compresses reasoning into a single token prediction that skips steps.

children.title: Zero-shot CoT & Step Triggers
children.description: Adding 'Let's think step by step' to a prompt activates chain-of-thought without needing explicit reasoning examples. Knowing which trigger phrases work on which model families saves significant prompt engineering time when moving across providers.

children.title: Self-Consistency Voting
children.description: Generating multiple reasoning paths and taking the majority answer substantially improves accuracy on hard reasoning tasks at the cost of more tokens. Understanding when to apply it — complex questions, not simple lookups — prevents wasting budget on tasks where one sample is enough.

children.title: Tree of Thought & Decomposition
children.description: Tree-of-thought extends chain-of-thought by exploring multiple reasoning branches and backtracking. For complex planning tasks it outperforms linear CoT, but requires careful prompt design to avoid exponential cost blowup.

resources.label: Prompt Engineering Guide — Tree of Thoughts

children.title: Reason-Act Interleaving
children.description: ReAct prompts the model to alternate between reasoning ('Thought:') and action ('Action:') steps, grounding decisions in real tool results rather than imagination. Without ReAct structure, tool-using agents frequently hallucinate tool outputs instead of waiting for real responses.

children.title: Reflection & Self-Critique
children.description: Asking the model to review and critique its own output before finalizing it catches surface-level errors that survive a single pass — wrong counts, broken formatting, factual inconsistencies. This technique is cheap and particularly valuable for code generation and structured data tasks.

children.title: Breaking Complex Tasks into Sub-tasks
children.description: Asking a single prompt to handle ten concerns simultaneously degrades reliability on all of them. Decomposing into focused sub-prompts — each with a single job — is the fastest practical way to improve output quality on complex pipelines.

children.title: Planning Prompts
children.description: Having the model produce an explicit plan or outline before executing it reduces mid-task drift and produces more coherent long-form outputs. Planning as a separate step is especially effective for multi-section documents, code architectures, and research summaries.

prompt-engineer.title: Structured Outputs & Function Calling
prompt-engineer.description: Make outputs machine-readable. Get reliable JSON and tool calls that integrate into real apps.
prompt-engineer.challenge: Design prompts and schemas that reliably extract structured data from messy text.

children.title: Requesting Structured Output
children.description: Models produce structured JSON far more reliably when given an explicit schema or skeleton to fill rather than a description of the schema. Skipping this causes type mismatches, missing keys, and nested-object errors that are expensive to detect and retry in production.

children.title: Validating & Repairing Output
children.description: Even with schema enforcement, outputs can fail validation due to context overflow, ambiguous fields, or model confidence degradation. Building a retry-with-error-feedback loop is essential infrastructure: re-prompt with the exact validation error to get clean output in one extra round-trip instead of crashing.

children.title: Defining Tool Schemas
children.description: Tool schemas are prompts themselves — the name, description, and parameter descriptions directly steer which tool the model calls and how it populates arguments. Vague descriptions produce wrong tool selection; precise ones make tool calls nearly deterministic.

children.title: Constrained Decoding
children.description: Libraries like Outlines and Instructor enforce grammar-level constraints during generation, making it structurally impossible to produce invalid JSON. This eliminates the entire class of parse-failure retries and is worth adopting as soon as your app processes structured output at volume.

children.title: Retry Strategies for Structured Output
children.description: Blind retries on structured output failures waste tokens and latency. Feeding the validation error back into the prompt — 'Your previous output was invalid because X, please correct it' — fixes the specific issue in one shot rather than hoping a random resample passes validation.

children.title: Fallback & Degradation Patterns
children.description: When structured extraction fails after retries, falling back to a simpler schema or a plain-text answer is better than returning an error to users. Designing your prompt system with explicit fallback tiers prevents a parsing edge case from cascading into a full system failure.

children.title: Flat vs Nested Schema Trade-offs
children.description: Deep nesting increases the probability of a model misplacing a field or hallucinating a required nested key. When reliability matters more than schema elegance, prefer flat structures and reconstruct nesting in post-processing code.

children.title: Optional vs Required Fields
children.description: Marking every field required forces the model to invent values when data is absent, producing confident hallucinations. Intentional optional fields signal to the model that omission is correct when information is missing, dramatically reducing fabrication.

prompt-engineer.title: RAG for Grounded Answers
prompt-engineer.description: Ground responses in real data. Learn how retrieval changes prompting and how to prompt over context.
prompt-engineer.challenge: Write prompts for a RAG system that answer strictly from context and say 'I don't know' when needed.

children.title: Hallucination vs Context-Bound Answers
children.description: A model answering from parametric memory will confidently produce plausible-sounding but outdated or fabricated facts. RAG constrains the model to a retrieved evidence set, converting an unreliable knowledge oracle into a reliable reading-comprehension engine.

children.title: Context Ordering & Recency Bias
children.description: LLMs exhibit primacy and recency bias — content at the start and end of the context window is weighted more than the middle. Placing the most critical retrieved passages at the top of the context and the instruction at the bottom leverages this bias rather than fighting it.

children.title: Context Formatting for Comprehension
children.description: How retrieved chunks are formatted in the prompt — with or without source labels, separated by delimiters, ordered by relevance — directly affects answer accuracy. Unlabeled or merged chunks cause the model to blend information across sources and lose attribution.

children.title: Instructing the Model to Cite Sources
children.description: Without explicit citation instructions, models summarize retrieved context without attribution, making it impossible to verify answers. A simple 'cite the document number for each claim' instruction produces traceable, auditable outputs that users and systems can trust.

children.title: Teaching the Model to Say 'I Don't Know'
children.description: By default models fill information gaps with plausible-sounding hallucinations. Adding an explicit instruction — 'If the answer is not in the provided context, respond with I don't have enough information to answer that' — is the single most impactful safety guardrail for RAG systems.

children.title: Conflict Resolution Across Documents
children.description: When retrieved documents contradict each other, a model without explicit instructions will silently pick one or blend them. Instructing the model to 'flag contradictions between sources' surfaces conflicts to users rather than hiding them in a confident but wrong synthesis.

children.title: Noise & Irrelevant Chunks
children.description: Injecting too much loosely related context degrades answer quality because the model attends to irrelevant passages and dilutes its focus on the correct evidence. Retrieval precision matters as much as prompt structure — garbage in, garbage out regardless of prompt quality.

children.title: Prompt-Context Boundary Clarity
children.description: When the boundary between instructions and retrieved content is blurry, models sometimes follow instructions embedded in the retrieved data rather than your system prompt. Explicit delimiters and position-of-instruction discipline prevent this class of context-injection bug.

resources.label: OWASP Top 10 for LLMs — Prompt Injection

prompt-engineer.title: Prompt Chaining & Workflows
prompt-engineer.description: Compose prompts into pipelines. Break complex tasks into chained steps with LangChain.
prompt-engineer.challenge: Build a multi-step prompt chain that drafts, critiques and revises a piece of writing.

children.title: Sequential Task Decomposition
children.description: Chaining breaks a complex job into a series of focused prompts where each output feeds the next input. This pattern improves reliability because each prompt handles one concern and failures are traceable to the specific step, not to a monolithic black box.

children.title: Managing Intermediate State
children.description: In a chain, intermediate outputs carry context forward — if step 2 output is malformed, step 3 silently propagates the error. Validating and sanitizing state between steps is the most common missing piece in home-grown chaining implementations.

children.title: LangChain Expression Language Basics
children.description: LCEL composes runnables with the pipe operator, making chains declarative and easy to swap components. Understanding how LCEL handles input/output mapping prevents the most common bug: passing the wrong key between chain steps.

resources.label: DeepLearning.AI — LangChain for LLM App Development

children.title: Routing & Conditional Flows
children.description: Real workflows require branching — routing to a specialized prompt based on classification output, or skipping steps when prior output meets a threshold. Without routing, you end up with one massive prompt trying to handle every case, which performs worse than specialized branches.

children.title: Map-Reduce Prompting
children.description: Map-reduce applies a prompt independently to each chunk of a large document, then reduces the results with a synthesis prompt. This bypasses context window limits and is the standard pattern for summarization, extraction, and classification over long inputs.

children.title: Combining Tools & Prompts
children.description: Hybrid pipelines interleave model calls with deterministic tool calls — search, calculation, database lookup — grounding each step in real data. The discipline here is deciding which steps must be a model call and which must be deterministic code, because using a model where code suffices adds latency and cost.

children.title: Multi-Pass Revision Chains
children.description: A chain that drafts, critiques its own draft, then revises produces substantially better outputs than a single-pass prompt for writing, code, and structured analysis. The key is making the critique step genuinely targeted — vague 'improve this' instructions produce shallow revisions.

children.title: Trace & Debug Chains
children.description: Without tracing, a broken five-step chain looks like a single bad output — you cannot tell which step failed. Integrating LangSmith or similar tracing from the start makes debugging a five-minute diff rather than a multi-hour investigation.

prompt-engineer.title: Evaluation & Testing Prompts
prompt-engineer.description: Prompts need testing like code. Build evaluation sets and measure whether changes actually help.
prompt-engineer.challenge: Create an eval set and use LLM-as-judge to compare two prompt versions objectively.

children.title: Curating Ground-Truth Test Sets
children.description: A prompt eval is only as good as its test set. Random samples and subjective vibes are not evaluation — you need a representative, labeled dataset with known-correct outputs. Without it you cannot tell if a prompt change improved the model or just changed how failures look.

children.title: Coverage & Edge Cases
children.description: Eval sets that only cover happy-path inputs report misleadingly high scores — your real failure rate is hidden in the edge cases you did not include. Deliberately adding hard negatives, ambiguous inputs, and adversarial examples produces honest metrics.

children.title: Using Models to Score Models
children.description: LLM-as-judge scales evaluation beyond what human reviewers can handle, but the judge prompt is a critical artifact that determines scoring reliability. A vague judge prompt produces noisy, biased scores; a precise rubric with examples produces scores that correlate with human preference.

children.title: Bias & Calibration of Judges
children.description: LLM judges exhibit position bias (preferring option A), verbosity bias (preferring longer answers), and self-enhancement bias (preferring their own provider's outputs). Calibrating your judge with human-labeled examples and using pairwise comparison reduces these systematic distortions.

children.title: Prompt Regression Suites
children.description: Every prompt change is a deployment — and just like code deployments, regressions happen. Running a fixed eval suite before and after every prompt modification catches silent performance drops that would otherwise only surface as user complaints weeks later.

children.title: A/B Testing Prompts
children.description: Running two prompt variants on the same inputs with the same judge gives a statistically grounded comparison, not a vibe-based preference. Without A/B infrastructure you cannot tell if a prompt is genuinely better or if you are cherry-picking favorable examples.

children.title: Task-Specific Metrics
children.description: Accuracy means different things for different tasks — BLEU for translation, F1 for extraction, faithfulness for RAG, pass@k for code. Using the wrong metric produces a prompt optimized for the metric rather than for the actual user need.

children.title: Human Feedback Loops
children.description: Automated metrics measure proxies; human feedback measures what actually matters. Building a lightweight thumbs-up/thumbs-down loop from the start creates a growing labeled dataset that improves both your evals and your prompts over time.

prompt-engineer.title: Guardrails & Safety
prompt-engineer.description: Make prompt systems safe. Defend against prompt injection and keep outputs within bounds.
prompt-engineer.challenge: Red-team your own prompt system for injection, then add guardrails that stop the attacks.

children.title: What Prompt Injection Is
children.description: Prompt injection occurs when user-controlled input overrides system instructions — essentially a SQL injection for LLMs. Every application that passes user text into a prompt is vulnerable unless the boundary between instructions and data is explicitly and structurally enforced.

resources.label: OWASP Top 10 for LLMs — Prompt Injection

children.title: System Prompt Hardening
children.description: Weak system prompts that say 'always be helpful' can be overridden by user instructions like 'ignore previous instructions'. Hardening involves explicit scope boundaries, refusal instructions, and structural isolation of user input using delimiters that the model is instructed not to parse as commands.

children.title: Input Validation & Sanitization
children.description: Validating and sanitizing user input before it enters the prompt — stripping control characters, detecting injection patterns, enforcing length limits — provides a deterministic first line of defense that does not depend on model behavior.

children.title: Output Moderation
children.description: Model outputs can include harmful content, PII, or confidential data even with a carefully written system prompt. Running outputs through a moderation layer — whether a classifier, a rules engine, or a guard model — before returning them to users is a non-negotiable production safety control.

children.title: Preventing PII Exposure in Prompts
children.description: Sending raw user data — names, emails, medical records — through LLM APIs creates compliance and privacy risks. Pseudonymizing PII before it enters the prompt and reversing it after output is the pattern that satisfies both GDPR/HIPAA requirements and LLM capability needs.

children.title: System Prompt Confidentiality
children.description: Users can often extract system prompts with social engineering prompts like 'repeat everything above'. Truly sensitive system prompts should not be treated as secrets — they will leak. Architecturally separating privileged logic into server-side code is more robust than relying on prompt instructions alone.

children.title: Designing Graceful Refusals
children.description: Blunt refusals that say 'I cannot help with that' frustrate users and erode trust. A good refusal explains what the system will not do, why in one sentence, and redirects to what it can do — maintaining helpfulness while enforcing the boundary.

children.title: Red-Teaming Your Prompts
children.description: Adversarially testing your own system before users do reveals injection vectors, jailbreak paths, and unexpected refusals that only appear under pressure. Structured red-teaming with a checklist of attack categories finds more issues in an hour than months of passive monitoring.

prompt-engineer.title: Optimization & Cost Control
prompt-engineer.description: Great prompts are also efficient. Reduce tokens, latency and cost without losing quality.
topics: Choosing the right model per task
prompt-engineer.challenge: Cut the token cost of a working prompt system by 40% while holding quality steady on your evals.

children.title: Counting & Measuring Token Cost
children.description: LLM cost is directly proportional to tokens in plus tokens out. Not measuring token consumption per prompt means you cannot predict or control API bills, and you will not know which prompt component is the biggest contributor to cost.

children.title: Prompt Compression Techniques
children.description: Long system prompts, verbose few-shot examples, and redundant instructions inflate cost without improving quality. Techniques like LLMLingua and selective few-shot trimming can cut prompt length by 30-50% with negligible quality loss, directly reducing cost per call.

children.title: Exact & Semantic Response Caching
children.description: Caching LLM responses for identical or semantically equivalent inputs eliminates redundant API calls and latency. Semantic caching — using embedding similarity to match near-identical queries — provides much higher hit rates than exact-match caching alone for conversational applications.

children.title: Prefix Caching & KV Cache
children.description: Anthropic and OpenAI both offer prefix/prompt caching that discounts repeated leading tokens. Structuring prompts to place static content (system prompt, documents) first and dynamic content last maximizes cache hit rate and can cut costs by up to 90% on read-heavy workloads.

children.title: Choosing the Right Model per Task
children.description: Using a frontier model for tasks a smaller, cheaper model handles adequately is one of the most common sources of unnecessary AI spend. Task-based routing — sending classification and extraction to small models and complex reasoning to frontier models — can cut costs by an order of magnitude.

children.title: Streaming to Reduce Perceived Latency
children.description: Streaming tokens to the UI as they are generated makes a five-second response feel interactive rather than frozen. Not streaming when latency matters is a user experience failure that is trivially cheap to fix — every major provider supports streaming in one line of code.

children.title: Measuring Quality Degradation at Lower Cost
children.description: Switching to a cheaper model or compressing a prompt without measuring quality impact is a false economy — you save tokens but lose accuracy in ways that only show up in production. Running your eval suite across cost levels gives you a quality-cost curve to make informed decisions.

children.title: Batching for Throughput
children.description: Batching multiple independent prompts into a single API call or using the Batch API reduces per-unit cost by up to 50% for offline workloads like bulk extraction, classification, and dataset generation. The trade-off is latency, which is acceptable for async pipelines.

prompt-engineer.title: Productionizing Prompt Systems
prompt-engineer.description: Operate prompts at scale. Version, deploy and monitor prompt systems like real software.
prompt-engineer.challenge: Set up versioned prompts with production monitoring and a safe rollout process for changes.

children.title: Treating Prompts as Code
children.description: A prompt in a string literal inside application code is unversioned, untested, and unauditable. Moving prompts into version-controlled templates with named versions lets you roll back changes, compare versions, and know exactly what prompt was running when a production issue occurred.

children.title: Parameterized Prompt Templates
children.description: Hard-coding values into prompts makes them impossible to reuse and easy to break. Parameterized templates with named slots — rendered at runtime with validated inputs — are the difference between a one-off script and a maintainable production system.

children.title: Logging Prompts & Responses
children.description: Without logging, you are flying blind — you cannot reproduce failures, measure quality drift, or audit what the model said to users. Structured logging of every prompt-response pair with metadata (model, latency, cost, user ID) is the foundation of operational visibility.

children.title: Quality Drift Detection
children.description: Model providers update models silently; user behavior changes over time; your prompt that worked last month may underperform today. Running your eval suite on a sample of live traffic weekly and alerting on metric drops catches drift before users file complaints.

children.title: Shadow Testing & Canary Deployments
children.description: Running a new prompt version in shadow mode — collecting its outputs without serving them — lets you evaluate production-quality behavior at zero user risk. Canary rollouts then gradually shift traffic to the new version while monitoring metrics, the same discipline applied to code deployments.

children.title: Rollback Procedures
children.description: When a prompt deployment causes a quality regression in production, the ability to revert to the previous version in under a minute is the difference between a minor incident and a major outage. Version-controlled prompts with a deployment registry make this a one-command operation.

children.title: Documenting Prompt Decisions
children.description: The rationale behind a prompt — why a particular phrasing was chosen, what failure it fixed, what trade-off was accepted — is lost without documentation. Future teammates (or future you) will spend hours rediscovering decisions that took minutes to make but weeks to validate.

children.title: Team Prompt Libraries
children.description: A shared, searchable library of battle-tested prompt components — system prompt templates, few-shot banks, output schemas — prevents each team member from reinventing the same solutions. Reuse compounds over time into a significant competitive advantage in shipping speed and quality.
